# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram: Linear Transformation Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    inputA["Input Vector x in R^n"] --> basisExpansion["Basis Expansion x equals sum c_i e_i"]
    basisExpansion --> linearityStep["Linearity T sum c_i e_i equals sum c_i T e_i"]
    linearityStep --> standardMatrix["Standard Matrix A m by n"]
    standardMatrix --> multiplication["Matrix Vector Product A x"]
    multiplication --> outputA["Output Vector y in R^m"]
    
    standardMatrix --> kernelCheck{"Kernel T empty?"}
    kernelCheck -- Yes --> injective["One to One Injective"]
    kernelCheck -- No --> notInjective["Not One to One"]
    
    standardMatrix --> rangeCheck{"Rank equals dim W?"}
    rangeCheck -- Yes --> surjective["Onto Surjective"]
    rangeCheck -- No --> notSurjective["Not Onto"]
    
    injective --> bijectiveCheck{"Also Onto?"}
    bijectiveCheck -- Yes --> invertible["Invertible Transformation"]
    bijectiveCheck -- No --> semiInj["Only Injective"]
    surjective --> semiInj2["Only Surjective"]
    notInjective --> none["No Special Property"]
    notSurjective --> none
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram: Rank-Nullity Theorem Visual Decomposition

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    dimV["Dimension of Domain dim V equals n"] --> decomposition["Total Decomposition"]
    decomposition --> nullityPart["Nullity Part dim ker T"]
    decomposition --> rankPart["Rank Part dim Range T"]
    
    nullityPart --> kernelSpace["Kernel Subspace of V\nVectors mapped to zero"]
    rankPart --> rangeSpace["Range Subspace of W\nAll possible outputs"]
    
    kernelSpace --> example1["Example: Projection onto x axis\nNull space is y axis in R^2"]
    rangeSpace --> example2["Example: Projection onto x axis\nRange is x axis in R^2"]
    
    example1 --> nullityVal["nullity T equals 1"]
    example2 --> rankVal["rank T equals 1"]
    
    nullityVal --> sum["Sum equals 1 plus 1 equals 2 equals dim R^2"]
    rankVal --> sum
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram: Composition of Linear Transformations

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    domainU["Domain U in R^p"] --> t1["T1: U to V\nMatrix A1 is m by p"]
    t1 --> codomainV["Intermediate Space V in R^m"]
    codomainV --> t2["T2: V to W\nMatrix A2 is n by m"]
    t2 --> codomainW["Final Codomain W in R^n"]
    
    t1 -.-> a1box["Stores images of standard basis of U as columns"]
    t2 -.-> a2box["Stores images of standard basis of V as columns"]
    a1box --> compose["Composition T2 circle T1: U to W\nStandard Matrix equals A2 times A1"]
    a2box --> compose
    compose --> result["Net Effect: One Single Matrix A2 A1\nTransforms vectors from R^p directly to R^n"]
```

## 4.4 Mermaid Diagram: Classification of Linear Transformations

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    start["Linear Transformation T: V to W"] --> q1{"Is Ker T equal to zero only?"}
    q1 -- Yes --> injective["INJECTIVE One to One\nDistinct inputs map to distinct outputs"]
    q1 -- No --> notInj["NOT INJECTIVE\nMultiple inputs collapse to same output"]
    
    injective --> q2{"Is Range T equal to W?"}
    notInj --> q2
    
    q2 -- Yes --> surjective["SURJECTIVE Onto\nEvery output is achievable"]
    q2 -- No --> notSurj["NOT SURJECTIVE\nSome outputs are unreachable"]
    
    surjective --> q3{"Both injective and surjective?"}
    notSurj --> q3
    
    q3 -- Yes --> bijective["BIJECTIVE / INVERTIBLE\nIsomorphism between V and W\nRequires dim V equals dim W"]
    q3 -- No --> other["Neither bijective nor full isomorphism\nBut still a valid linear map"]
    
    bijective --> inv["Inverse T inverse exists\nSatisfies T inverse circle T equals Identity V"]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagrams:** The arrows indicate the *flow of computation* — from input vector, through basis expansion, application of linearity, and finally to matrix-vector multiplication. The diamond nodes represent decision points based on the kernel/range analysis, which is exactly how the KTU examiner expects you to classify any given linear transformation.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define a Linear Transformation. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]

**Model Answer:**

A linear transformation $T: V \to W$ between two vector spaces $V$ and $W$ over a field $\mathbb{F}$ is a function that preserves the two fundamental vector space operations: vector addition and scalar multiplication. Formally, for all $\mathbf{u}, \mathbf{v} \in V$ and all scalars $c \in \mathbb{F}$:

- $T(\mathbf{u} + \mathbf{v}) = T(\mathbf{u}) + T(\mathbf{v})$ (preservation of addition)
- $T(c\mathbf{u}) = cT(\mathbf{u})$ (preservation of scalar multiplication)

Equivalently, $T$ preserves linear combinations: $T(a\mathbf{u} + b\mathbf{v}) = aT(\mathbf{u}) + bT(\mathbf{v})$ for all $a, b \in \mathbb{F}$.

**Example:** The map $T: \mathbb{R}^2 \to \mathbb{R}^2$ defined by $T(x, y) = (2x, 3y)$ is linear, since $T(au + bv) = (2(au_1 + bv_1), 3(au_2 + bv_2)) = a(2u_1, 3u_2) + b(2v_1, 3v_2) = aT(u) + bT(v)$.

**Counter-example:** $T(x, y) = (x + 1, y)$ is **not** linear because $T(\mathbf{0}) = (1, 0) \neq \mathbf{0}$.

---

### Question 2: State and Explain the Rank-Nullity Theorem. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]

**Model Answer:**

**Statement:** If $T: V \to W$ is a linear transformation from an $n$-dimensional vector space $V$ to any vector space $W$, then:

$$
\dim(V) = \text{rank}(T) + \text{nullity}(T)
$$

where $\text{rank}(T) = \dim(\text{Range}(T))$ and $\text{nullity}(T) = \dim(\ker(T))$.

**Explanation:** The theorem partitions the dimension of the domain $V$ into two complementary parts: vectors that contribute to the "useful" output (range) and vectors that get crushed to zero (kernel). For an $m \times n$ matrix $A$, this becomes $n = \text{rank}(A) + \text{nullity}(A)$.

**Example:** For $A = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 2 & 1 \\ 2 & 4 & 2 \end{bmatrix}$, row-reducing gives one pivot, so $\text{rank}(A) = 1$ and $\text{nullity}(A) = 3 - 1 = 2$. Sum: $1 + 2 = 3 = n$. ✓

---

## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks): [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]

**(a)** Define a linear transformation. Find the standard matrix $A$ of the linear transformation $T: \mathbb{R}^3 \to \mathbb{R}^2$ defined by $T(x, y, z) = (2x - 3y + z,\ x + 2y - 4z)$. Also, find $T(1, -2, 3)$. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Find the kernel, range, rank, and nullity of the linear transformation with standard matrix:

$$
A = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 1 & 1 \\ 1 & 2 & 3 \\ 2 & 3 & 4 \end{bmatrix}
$$

Verify the Rank-Nullity Theorem. **(7 marks)**

---

**Solution to (a):**

**Definition (1 mark):** A linear transformation $T: V \to W$ satisfies $T(a\mathbf{u} + b\mathbf{v}) = aT(\mathbf{u}) + bT(\mathbf{v})$ for all $\mathbf{u}, \mathbf{v} \in V$ and $a, b \in \mathbb{F}$.

**Standard Matrix Construction (4 marks):** Compute $T$ on the standard basis vectors:

For $\mathbf{e}_1 = (1, 0, 0)$:

$$
T(\mathbf{e}_1) = (2(1) - 0 + 0,\ 1 + 0 - 0) = (2, 1)
$$

For $\mathbf{e}_2 = (0, 1, 0)$:

$$
T(\mathbf{e}_2) = (0 - 3 + 0,\ 0 + 2 - 0) = (-3, 2)
$$

For $\mathbf{e}_3 = (0, 0, 1)$:

$$
T(\mathbf{e}_3) = (0 - 0 + 1,\ 0 + 0 - 4) = (1, -4)
$$

Therefore:

$$
A = \begin{bmatrix} 2 & -3 & 1 \\ 1 & 2 & -4 \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Apply to Vector (2 marks):** For $\mathbf{x} = (1, -2, 3)^T$:

$$
T(1, -2, 3) = A \begin{pmatrix} 1 \\ -2 \\ 3 \end{pmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 2 & -3 & 1 \\ 1 & 2 & -4 \end{bmatrix} \begin{pmatrix} 1 \\ -2 \\ 3 \end{pmatrix}
$$

$$
= \begin{pmatrix} 2(1) + (-3)(-2) + 1(3) \\ 1(1) + 2(-2) + (-4)(3) \end{pmatrix} = \begin{pmatrix} 2 + 6 + 3 \\ 1 - 4 - 12 \end{pmatrix} = \begin{pmatrix} 11 \\ -15 \end{pmatrix}
$$

**Direct check:** $T(1, -2, 3) = (2(1) - 3(-2) + 3,\ 1 + 2(-2) - 4(3)) = (2 + 6 + 3,\ 1 - 4 - 12) = (11, -15)$ ✓

---

**Solution to (b):**

**Row Reduction (3 marks):**

$$
A = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 1 & 1 \\ 1 & 2 & 3 \\ 2 & 3 & 4 \end{bmatrix} \xrightarrow{R_2 \to R_2 - R_1} \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 1 & 1 \\ 0 & 1 & 2 \\ 2 & 3 & 4 \end{bmatrix}
$$

$$
\xrightarrow{R_3 \to R_3 - 2R_1} \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 1 & 1 \\ 0 & 1 & 2 \\ 0 & 1 & 2 \end{bmatrix} \xrightarrow{R_3 \to R_3 - R_2} \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 1 & 1 \\ 0 & 1 & 2 \\ 0 & 0 & 0 \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Rank and Nullity (1 mark):** Two pivots $\Rightarrow \text{rank}(A) = 2$, $\text{nullity}(A) = 3 - 2 = 1$.

**Kernel (2 marks):** From the echelon form, with $x_3 = t$ (free):

From row 2: $x_2 + 2x_3 = 0 \Rightarrow x_2 = -2t$.
From row 1: $x_1 + x_2 + x_3 = 0 \Rightarrow x_1 = -x_2 - x_3 = 2t - t = t$.

$$
\mathbf{x} = t \begin{pmatrix} 1 \\ -2 \\ 1 \end{pmatrix}, \quad \ker(T) = \text{span}\!\left\{ \begin{pmatrix} 1 \\ -2 \\ 1 \end{pmatrix} \right\}
$$

**Range (1 mark):** Pivot columns are 1 and 2. The range is spanned by the corresponding columns of the **original** $A$:

$$
R(T) = \text{span}\!\left\{ \begin{pmatrix} 1 \\ 1 \\ 2 \end{pmatrix},\ \begin{pmatrix} 1 \\ 2 \\ 3 \end{pmatrix} \right\}
$$

**Verification (1 mark):** $\dim(V) = 3 = \text{rank}(T) + \text{nullity}(T) = 2 + 1$ ✓

---

### Question B (14 Marks): [KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Alternative]

**(a)** Determine whether the linear transformation $T: \mathbb{R}^2 \to \mathbb{R}^2$ given by $T(x, y) = (4x - 2y,\ 2x - y)$ is one-to-one, onto, and invertible. Justify your answer using the kernel and determinant. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Two linear transformations are defined as $T_1: \mathbb{R}^2 \to \mathbb{R}^3$ with $T_1(x, y) = (x + 2y,\ 3x - y,\ 2x + y)$ and $T_2: \mathbb{R}^3 \to \mathbb{R}^3$ with $T_2(a, b, c) = (a + b,\ b - c,\ a + 2c)$. Find the standard matrix of $T_2 \circ T_1$. Is $T_2 \circ T_1$ invertible? Justify. **(7 marks)**

---

**Solution to (a):**

**Standard Matrix (1 mark):**

$$
A = \begin{bmatrix} 4 & -2 \\ 2 & -1 \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Determinant Test (2 marks):**

$$
\det(A) = (4)(-1) - (-2)(2) = -4 + 4 = 0
$$

**Kernel Analysis (2 marks):** Since $\det(A) = 0$, $A\mathbf{x} = \mathbf{0}$ has non-trivial solutions. Row reduce:

$$
A = \begin{bmatrix} 4 & -2 \\ 2 & -1 \end{bmatrix} \xrightarrow{R_2 \to 2R_2 - R_1} \begin{bmatrix} 4 & -2 \\ 0 & 0 \end{bmatrix}
$$

From $4x_1 - 2x_2 = 0 \Rightarrow x_2 = 2x_1$. Let $x_1 = t$:

$$
\ker(T) = \text{span}\!\left\{ \begin{pmatrix} 1 \\ 2 \end{pmatrix} \right\}, \quad \text{nullity}(T) = 1 \neq 0
$$

**Conclusions (2 marks):**
- **Not one-to-one:** $\ker(T) \neq \{\mathbf{0}\}$ (specifically, $(1, 2)^T$ is a non-zero vector mapped to zero).
- **Not onto:** $\text{rank}(T) = 1 < \dim(\mathbb{R}^2) = 2$.
- **Not invertible:** $\det(A) = 0$, so $A^{-1}$ does not exist.

---

**Solution to (b):**

**Standard Matrices (2 marks):**

$$
A_1 = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 2 \\ 3 & -1 \\ 2 & 1 \end{bmatrix}, \quad A_2 = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 1 & 0 \\ 0 & 1 & -1 \\ 1 & 0 & 2 \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Compute $A_2 A_1$ (3 marks):**

$$
A_2 A_1 = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 1 & 0 \\ 0 & 1 & -1 \\ 1 & 0 & 2 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 2 \\ 3 & -1 \\ 2 & 1 \end{bmatrix}
$$

Compute entries:
- $(1,1)$: $(1)(1) + (1)(3) + (0)(2) = 4$
- $(1,2)$: $(1)(2) + (1)(-1) + (0)(1) = 1$
- $(2,1)$: $(0)(1) + (1)(3) + (-1)(2) = 1$
- $(2,2)$: $(0)(2) + (1)(-1) + (-1)(1) = -2$
- $(3,1)$: $(1)(1) + (0)(3) + (2)(2) = 5$
- $(3,2)$: $(1)(2) + (0)(-1) + (2)(1) = 4$

Therefore:

$$
A_2 A_1 = \begin{bmatrix} 4 & 1 \\ 1 & -2 \\ 5 & 4 \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Invertibility Check (2 marks):** The matrix $A_2 A_1$ is $3 \times 2$, which is **not square**. A non-square matrix cannot be invertible (the composition $T_2 \circ T_1$ maps $\mathbb{R}^2$ to $\mathbb{R}^3$, so the dimensions are unequal). Therefore, $T_2 \circ T_1$ is **not invertible**.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting to use ORIGINAL columns for Range:** Many students use the pivot columns of the *reduced* matrix instead of the *original* $A$. This is wrong and will cost you 1–2 marks.
> 2. **Confusing $\ker(T)$ with the entire solution set of $A\mathbf{x} = \mathbf{0}$:** The kernel is the *subspace* (including the zero vector), not just the non-trivial solutions.
> 3. **Order of Composition:** When asked for $T_2 \circ T_1$, the matrix is $A_2 A_1$ (rightmost applied first). Reversing the order is a fatal error.
> 4. **Nullity Computation:** A common mistake is counting pivots as nullity. The nullity is $n - (\text{number of pivots})$, where $n$ is the number of columns.
> 5. **Forgetting to verify Rank-Nullity:** Even if you compute rank and nullity correctly, the KTU examiner expects an **explicit verification** statement, such as "$3 = 1 + 2$ ✓". This can fetch you 1 bonus mark.
> 6. **Sign errors in Determinant:** Remember the formula $\det \begin{bmatrix} a & b \\ c & d \end{bmatrix} = ad - bc$, not $ad + bc$.

---

## 📌 TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER

- **Definition:** $T: V \to W$ is linear if $T(a\mathbf{u} + b\mathbf{v}) = aT(\mathbf{u}) + bT(\mathbf{v})$.
- **Standard Matrix:** Columns are $T(\mathbf{e}_i)$ for $i = 1, \ldots, n$.
- **Kernel:** $\ker(T) = \{\mathbf{v} \in V : T(\mathbf{v}) = \mathbf{0}\}$ — always a subspace of $V$.
- **Range:** $R(T) = \{T(\mathbf{v}) : \mathbf{v} \in V\}$ — always a subspace of $W$.
- **Rank-Nullity Theorem:** $\dim(V) = \text{rank}(T) + \text{nullity}(T)$ — *tested in nearly every KTU exam*.
- **One-to-One:** $\iff \ker(T) = \{\mathbf{0}\}$ $\iff$ columns of $A$ are linearly independent.
- **Onto:** $\iff R(T) = W$ $\iff \text{rank}(A) = \dim(W)$.
- **Invertible:** $\iff$ square matrix with $\det(A) \neq 0$ $\iff$ both one-to-one and onto.
- **Composition:** $T_2 \circ T_1$ has matrix $A_2 A_1$ (apply $A_1$ first, then $A_2$).
- **Inverse of Composition:** $(T_2 \circ T_1)^{-1} = T_1^{-1} \circ T_2^{-1}$ (order reverses).
- **Zero Preservation:** $T(\mathbf{0}) = \mathbf{0}$ is a *necessary* condition for linearity, not sufficient — always check additivity separately.
- **Geometric Transformations:** Rotation, reflection, projection, and scaling are all linear maps with well-known matrix forms.
- **ML Connection:** Weight matrices in neural networks are standard matrices of linear transformations. PCA uses projection (a linear map) onto a lower-dimensional subspace.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Last-Minute Mnemonic (KTU Board Favorite):** "**KARO**" — **K**ernel, **A**dd to, **R**ange, **O**utput. $\dim(V) = \dim(\ker) + \dim(\text{Range})$.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Change of Basis and Similarity
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAET753 - LINEAR ALGEBRA IN AI AND ML
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Change of Basis and Similarity

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Change of Basis (KTU 2024 Scheme Definition):** Let $V$ be a finite-dimensional vector space over a field $\mathbb{F}$ (typically $\mathbb{R}$ or $\mathbb{C}$). Suppose $\mathcal{B} = \{\mathbf{b}_1, \mathbf{b}_2, \ldots, \mathbf{b}_n\}$ and $\mathcal{B}' = \{\mathbf{b}'_1, \mathbf{b}'_2, \ldots, \mathbf{b}'_n\}$ are two ordered bases of $V$. The **change of basis** is the invertible linear transformation that converts the coordinate vector of any $\mathbf{v} \in V$ expressed in basis $\mathcal{B}$ to its coordinate vector expressed in basis $\mathcal{B}'$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Matrix Similarity (KTU 2024 Scheme Definition):** Two square matrices $A, B \in \mathbb{F}^{n \times n}$ are said to be **similar** if there exists an invertible matrix $P \in GL(n, \mathbb{F})$ such that $B = P^{-1}AP$. The transformation $A \mapsto P^{-1}AP$ is called a **similarity transformation**.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — The Universal Translator:**
> Imagine you have a GPS coordinate system. The same physical location "Kerala, India" can be expressed in **(latitude, longitude)** or in **(UTM grid coordinates)** or even in **(Google Map X,Y pixels)**. The location hasn't moved — only the **coordinate system (basis)** has changed. **Change of basis** is precisely this translation rule between coordinate languages.

> **Similarity Analogy — The Same Movie in Different Formats:**
> A movie stored in `.mp4`, `.avi`, or `.mkv` contains identical content. The *file format* (representation matrix) looks completely different, but the *information* (linear operator) is unchanged. The conversion rule between formats is the matrix $P$. This is exactly what similarity captures: **same linear operator, different matrix representation**.

### 1.3 Why This Topic Matters in AI/ML

> [!NOTE]
> In AI/ML, similarity transformations are the mathematical engine behind:
> - **Principal Component Analysis (PCA):** Diagonalizing the covariance matrix
> - **Eigenface recognition:** Basis transformation into eigenface space
> - **Neural network initialization:** Orthogonal/similarity-preserving weight matrices
> - **Quantum ML:** Unitary similarity gates

**Key Quantities (KTU Board Expects):**
- Basis vector: dimensionless (just a direction)
- Change of basis matrix: $P \in GL(n, \mathbb{R})$ or $GL(n, \mathbb{C})$
- Similarity invariant quantities: **determinant**, **trace**, **rank**, **eigenvalues**, **minimal polynomial**
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Logic — Step by Step

**Step 1: Coordinate Representation**
Any vector $\mathbf{v}$ has two distinct coordinate representations:
$$[\mathbf{v}]_{\mathcal{B}} = \begin{bmatrix} c_1 \\ c_2 \\ \vdots \\ c_n \end{bmatrix}, \quad [\mathbf{v}]_{\mathcal{B}'} = \begin{bmatrix} c'_1 \\ c'_2 \\ \vdots \\ c'_n \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 2: Building the Change of Basis Matrix**
Express each new basis vector $\mathbf{b}'_j$ in terms of the old basis $\{\mathbf{b}_i\}$:
$$\mathbf{b}'_j = \sum_{i=1}^{n} p_{ij}\, \mathbf{b}_i$$

**Step 3: Matrix Form**
The columns of $P$ are precisely these coordinates:
$$P = \begin{bmatrix} [\mathbf{b}'_1]_{\mathcal{B}} & [\mathbf{b}'_2]_{\mathcal{B}} & \cdots & [\mathbf{b}'_n]_{\mathcal{B}} \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 4: The Two Directional Transformations**
- **Old basis $\rightarrow$ New basis coordinates:** $[\mathbf{v}]_{\mathcal{B}'} = P^{-1}[\mathbf{v}]_{\mathcal{B}}$
- **New basis $\rightarrow$ Old basis coordinates:** $[\mathbf{v}]_{\mathcal{B}} = P[\mathbf{v}]_{\mathcal{B}'}$

**Step 5: Why "How" Works**
Because the *same* vector $\mathbf{v}$ satisfies both:
$$\mathbf{v} = [\mathbf{v}]_{\mathcal{B}}^T (\mathbf{b}_1, \ldots, \mathbf{b}_n) = [\mathbf{v}]_{\mathcal{B}'}^T (\mathbf{b}'_1, \ldots, \mathbf{b}'_n)$$

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following table is **board-exam critical**. Memorize all rows.

| # | Concept | Formula | Conditions / Notes |
|---|---------|---------|--------------------|
| 1 | Change of basis (B → B') | $[\mathbf{v}]_{\mathcal{B}'} = P^{-1}[\mathbf{v}]_{\mathcal{B}}$ | $P = \begin{bmatrix} [\mathbf{b}'_1]_\mathcal{B} \cdots [\mathbf{b}'_n]_\mathcal{B} \end{bmatrix}$ |
| 2 | Inverse direction | $[\mathbf{v}]_{\mathcal{B}} = P[\mathbf{v}]_{\mathcal{B}'}$ | $P$ must be invertible |
| 3 | Similarity definition | $B = P^{-1}AP$ | $P \in GL(n)$ |
| 4 | Inverse similarity | $A = PBP^{-1}$ | Symmetric equivalence |
| 5 | Similarity invariants | $\det(B) = \det(A)$ | Same determinant |
| 6 | Trace invariant | $\operatorname{tr}(B) = \operatorname{tr}(A)$ | Same trace |
| 7 | Eigenvalue invariant | $\sigma(B) = \sigma(A)$ | Identical spectrum |
| 8 | Rank invariant | $\operatorname{rank}(B) = \operatorname{rank}(A)$ | Equal rank |
| 9 | Characteristic polynomial | $p_B(\lambda) = p_A(\lambda)$ | Unchanged |
| 10 | Transpose of similarity | $B^T = P^T A^T (P^T)^{-1}$ | $P^T \in GL(n)$ |
| 11 | Power rule | $B^k = P^{-1}A^k P$ | $k \in \mathbb{Z}^+$ |
| 12 | Diagonalization | $A = PDP^{-1}$ | $D$ diagonal, $P$ eigenvector matrix |
| 13 | Orthogonal similarity | $B = Q^{-1}AQ$ with $Q^T Q = I$ | Preserves lengths |
| 14 | Determinant of $P$ | $\det(P) \neq 0$ | Required for invertibility |

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In **robotics**, changing basis converts joint-space coordinates to Cartesian end-effector coordinates. In **computer graphics**, the View Matrix is exactly a change-of-basis matrix that maps world coordinates to camera coordinates. In **ML/AI**, similarity diagonalization reduces covariance matrices into interpretable principal axes — this is the mathematical heart of **PCA**.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: The Change of Basis Formula

**Setup:** Let $\mathcal{B} = \{\mathbf{e}_1, \mathbf{e}_2\}$ and $\mathcal{B}' = \{\mathbf{f}_1, \mathbf{f}_2\}$ be two bases of $\mathbb{R}^2$.

Suppose the new basis vectors are expressed in the old basis as:
$$\mathbf{f}_1 = 2\mathbf{e}_1 + 1\mathbf{e}_2, \quad \mathbf{f}_2 = 3\mathbf{e}_1 + 4\mathbf{e}_2$$

**Step 1:** Build the change of basis matrix $P$ by placing the new basis vectors as columns (in the OLD basis coordinates):
$$P = \begin{bmatrix} 2 & 3 \\ 1 & 4 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 2:** Let $\mathbf{v} = 7\mathbf{e}_1 + 9\mathbf{e}_2$, so $[\mathbf{v}]_\mathcal{B} = \begin{bmatrix} 7 \\ 9 \end{bmatrix}$.

**Step 3:** Compute $P^{-1}$ explicitly:
$$\det(P) = (2)(4) - (3)(1) = 8 - 3 = 5$$

$$P^{-1} = \frac{1}{5}\begin{bmatrix} 4 & -3 \\ -1 & 2 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 4:** Convert coordinates:
$$[\mathbf{v}]_{\mathcal{B}'} = P^{-1}[\mathbf{v}]_{\mathcal{B}} = \frac{1}{5}\begin{bmatrix} 4 & -3 \\ -1 & 2 \end{bmatrix}\begin{bmatrix} 7 \\ 9 \end{bmatrix}$$

$$= \frac{1}{5}\begin{bmatrix} 28 - 27 \\ -7 + 18 \end{bmatrix} = \frac{1}{5}\begin{bmatrix} 1 \\ 11 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 1/5 \\ 11/5 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 5: Verification (reverse direction):**
$$P[\mathbf{v}]_{\mathcal{B}'} = \begin{bmatrix} 2 & 3 \\ 1 & 4 \end{bmatrix}\begin{bmatrix} 1/5 \\ 11/5 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 2/5 + 33/5 \\ 1/5 + 44/5 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 35/5 \\ 45/5 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 7 \\ 9 \end{bmatrix} \;\checkmark$$

This confirms $[\mathbf{v}]_{\mathcal{B}}$ is recovered.

---

### 3.2 Derivation: Similarity Invariance of Eigenvalues

**Claim:** If $B = P^{-1}AP$, then $\sigma(B) = \sigma(A)$.

**Step 1:** Write the characteristic polynomial of $B$:
$$p_B(\lambda) = \det(\lambda I - B)$$

**Step 2:** Substitute $B = P^{-1}AP$:
$$p_B(\lambda) = \det(\lambda I - P^{-1}AP)$$

**Step 3:** Factor out $P^{-1}$ on the left and $P$ on the right using the identity $P^{-1}P = I$:
$$= \det(P^{-1}(\lambda I) P - P^{-1}AP)$$

**Step 4:** Since $P^{-1}(\lambda I)P = \lambda P^{-1}P = \lambda I$:
$$= \det(P^{-1}(\lambda I - A)P)$$

**Step 5:** Apply multiplicativity of determinant $\det(XY) = \det(X)\det(Y)$:
$$= \det(P^{-1}) \cdot \det(\lambda I - A) \cdot \det(P)$$

**Step 6:** Use $\det(P^{-1})\det(P) = 1$:
$$p_B(\lambda) = \det(\lambda I - A) = p_A(\lambda)$$

**Conclusion:** The characteristic polynomials are identical, therefore the eigenvalues (roots) are identical. $\blacksquare$

---

### 3.3 Worked Example — Full Similarity Transformation

**Problem (KTU-style):** Let
$$A = \begin{bmatrix} 4 & 1 \\ 2 & 3 \end{bmatrix}$$

Find a similar matrix $B$ using
$$P = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 1 \\ 1 & 2 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 1: Verify $P$ is invertible.**
$$\det(P) = (1)(2) - (1)(1) = 1 \neq 0 \;\checkmark$$

**Step 2: Compute $P^{-1}$.**
$$P^{-1} = \frac{1}{1}\begin{bmatrix} 2 & -1 \\ -1 & 1 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 2 & -1 \\ -1 & 1 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 3: Compute $AP$.**
$$AP = \begin{bmatrix} 4 & 1 \\ 2 & 3 \end{bmatrix}\begin{bmatrix} 1 & 1 \\ 1 & 2 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 4+1 & 4+2 \\ 2+3 & 2+6 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 5 & 6 \\ 5 & 8 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 4: Compute $B = P^{-1}(AP)$.**
$$B = \begin{bmatrix} 2 & -1 \\ -1 & 1 \end{bmatrix}\begin{bmatrix} 5 & 6 \\ 5 & 8 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 10-5 & 12-8 \\ -5+5 & -6+8 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 5 & 4 \\ 0 & 2 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 5: Verify invariants.**
- $\det(A) = (4)(3) - (1)(2) = 12 - 2 = 10$
- $\det(B) = (5)(2) - (4)(0) = 10 \;\checkmark$
- $\operatorname{tr}(A) = 4 + 3 = 7$
- $\operatorname{tr}(B) = 5 + 2 = 7 \;\checkmark$

> [!NOTE]
> Notice how $B$ is in **upper triangular form**. This is no coincidence: the diagonal entries of $B$ are exactly the **eigenvalues** of $A$ (solve $\det(A - \lambda I) = \lambda^2 - 7\lambda + 10 = (\lambda-5)(\lambda-2) = 0$, giving $\lambda = 5, 2$). This is the gateway to **diagonalization**.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Diagram: Change of Basis Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["Vector v in V"] --> B["Coordinates in basis B"]
    A --> C["Coordinates in basis B prime"]
    B -- "P matrix multiply" --> D["Coordinate form 1"]
    C -- "P inverse multiply" --> E["Coordinate form 2"]
    D --> F["Verify equal representation"]
    E --> F
    F --> G["Same physical vector v"]
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Diagram: Similarity Transformation Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    O["Original matrix A"] --> T["Similarity transform P inverse A P"]
    T --> S["Similar matrix B"]
    S --> I1["Same determinant"]
    S --> I2["Same trace"]
    S --> I3["Same eigenvalues"]
    S --> I4["Same rank"]
    S --> I5["Same characteristic polynomial"]
    O --> I1
    O --> I2
    O --> I3
    O --> I4
    O --> I5
    P["Invertible P"] --> T
    Pinv["P inverse"] --> T
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Diagram: Diagonalization Pathway (Application of Similarity)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    M["Matrix A n x n"] --> E["Find eigenvalues"]
    M --> V["Find eigenvectors"]
    E --> D["Form diagonal D"]
    V --> P["Form eigenvector matrix P"]
    D --> R["A = P D P inverse"]
    P --> R
    Pinv2["P inverse"] --> R
    R --> APP["ML application: PCA, Eigendecomposition"]
```

### 4.4 Mermaid Diagram: Invariance Hierarchy

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    L1["Similarity B = P inverse A P"] --> L2["Spectral invariants preserved"]
    L2 --> L3["Eigenvalues"]
    L2 --> L4["Determinant"]
    L2 --> L5["Trace"]
    L2 --> L6["Rank"]
    L2 --> L7["Characteristic polynomial"]
    L2 --> L8["Minimal polynomial"]
    L2 --> L9["Jordan canonical form"]
    L3 --> A["Engineering use: stable system design"]
    L4 --> A
    L5 --> A
    L6 --> A
    L7 --> A
    L8 --> A
    L9 --> A
```

> [!NOTE]
> The diagrams above are **board-exam ready**. The KTU 2024 Scheme frequently tests the *invariance hierarchy* — the deeper the invariant, the more rigid the similarity class.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*Define change of basis. State the formula relating the coordinate vectors of a vector in two different bases.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Change of basis is the invertible linear transformation that converts coordinates of a vector expressed in one basis to coordinates in another basis. If $\mathcal{B}$ and $\mathcal{B}'$ are two bases and $P = \begin{bmatrix} [\mathbf{b}'_1]_\mathcal{B} \cdots [\mathbf{b}'_n]_\mathcal{B} \end{bmatrix}$, then:
$$[\mathbf{v}]_{\mathcal{B}'} = P^{-1}[\mathbf{v}]_{\mathcal{B}}$$

**Distribution:** Definition (1) + Formula (1) + Statement of $P$ (1) = **3 marks**

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
*State the definition of similar matrices. List any four properties preserved under similarity.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Two matrices $A, B \in \mathbb{F}^{n \times n}$ are similar if there exists an invertible $P$ such that $B = P^{-1}AP$.

Four preserved properties:
1. Determinant: $\det(B) = \det(A)$
2. Trace: $\operatorname{tr}(B) = \operatorname{tr}(A)$
3. Eigenvalues (spectrum): $\sigma(B) = \sigma(A)$
4. Rank: $\operatorname{rank}(B) = \operatorname{rank}(A)$

**Distribution:** Definition (1) + Four properties (0.5 each) = **3 marks**

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Read Before Attempting!**
> 1. **Forgetting to verify invertibility of $P$** before declaring $B$ similar to $A$: If $\det(P) = 0$, $P^{-1}$ does not exist — instant 0 marks.
> 2. **Confusing $P^{-1}AP$ with $PAP^{-1}$**: This reverses similarity direction and is a common 2–3 mark deduction.
> 3. **Skipping invariant verification**: KTU 2024 Scheme mandates that you verify $\det$, $\operatorname{tr}$, or eigenvalues to *prove* similarity. Not verifying costs 2 marks.
> 4. **Sign errors in $P^{-1}$**: Use the formula $P^{-1} = \frac{1}{\det(P)}\operatorname{adj}(P)$ — ad-jugate, not cofactor matrix directly.
> 5. **Forgetting to mention field assumptions**: Similarity is field-dependent; state $\mathbb{R}$ or $\mathbb{C}$ explicitly.

---

**Q3. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2024, Model Question]**
**Question A (14 marks):**
(a) **(7 marks)** Let $\mathcal{B} = \{(1,0), (0,1)\}$ and $\mathcal{B}' = \{(1,1), (1,-1)\}$ be two bases of $\mathbb{R}^2$. Find the change of basis matrix $P$ from $\mathcal{B}$ to $\mathcal{B}'$. Hence, find the coordinates of $\mathbf{v} = (3, 4)$ with respect to $\mathcal{B}'$.
(b) **(7 marks)** Verify your answer by transforming back from $\mathcal{B}'$ to $\mathcal{B}$.

### Model Solution — Question A

**Part (a) — 7 marks:**

**Step 1:** Write new basis vectors in old basis coordinates:
- $\mathbf{b}'_1 = (1,1) = 1\cdot(1,0) + 1\cdot(0,1) \Rightarrow [\mathbf{b}'_1]_\mathcal{B} = \begin{bmatrix} 1 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix}$
- $\mathbf{b}'_2 = (1,-1) = 1\cdot(1,0) + (-1)\cdot(0,1) \Rightarrow [\mathbf{b}'_2]_\mathcal{B} = \begin{bmatrix} 1 \\ -1 \end{bmatrix}$

**Step 2:** Form $P$:
$$P = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 1 \\ 1 & -1 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 3:** Compute $\det(P) = (1)(-1) - (1)(1) = -1 - 1 = -2 \neq 0$ (invertible ✓)

**Step 4:** Compute $P^{-1}$:
$$P^{-1} = \frac{1}{-2}\begin{bmatrix} -1 & -1 \\ -1 & 1 \end{bmatrix} = \frac{1}{2}\begin{bmatrix} 1 & 1 \\ 1 & -1 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 5:** $[\mathbf{v}]_\mathcal{B} = \begin{bmatrix} 3 \\ 4 \end{bmatrix}$, so:
$$[\mathbf{v}]_{\mathcal{B}'} = P^{-1}[\mathbf{v}]_\mathcal{B} = \frac{1}{2}\begin{bmatrix} 1 & 1 \\ 1 & -1 \end{bmatrix}\begin{bmatrix} 3 \\ 4 \end{bmatrix} = \frac{1}{2}\begin{bmatrix} 7 \\ -1 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 7/2 \\ -1/2 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Marks Distribution:** Express new basis (2) + Form $P$ (1) + Invert $P$ (2) + Final answer (2) = **7 marks**

**Part (b) — 7 marks:**

**Step 1:** Apply reverse transformation:
$$[\mathbf{v}]_\mathcal{B = P[\mathbf{v}]_{\mathcal{B}'}} = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 1 \\ 1 & -1 \end{bmatrix}\begin{bmatrix} 7/2 \\ -1/2 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 7/2 - 1/2 \\ 7/2 + 1/2 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 3 \\ 4 \end{bmatrix} \;\checkmark$$

**Step 2:** Verify by reconstruction: $\mathbf{v} = \frac{7}{2}(1,1) + (-\frac{1}{2})(1,-1) = (\frac{7}{2} - \frac{1}{2}, \frac{7}{2} + \frac{1}{2}) = (3, 4)$ ✓

**Marks Distribution:** Forward computation (3) + Backward verification (2) + Reconstruction check (2) = **7 marks**

---

**Question B (14 marks) — Alternative Choice:**
(a) **(7 marks)** Show that the matrices
$$A = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 2 \\ 0 & 3 \end{bmatrix}, \quad B = \begin{bmatrix} 3 & 2 \\ 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}$$
are similar by finding an invertible matrix $P$ such that $B = P^{-1}AP$. (Hint: Try $P$ with $\det P = -1$.)
(b) **(7 marks)** Verify the similarity by checking two invariants.

### Model Solution — Question B

**Part (a) — 7 marks:**

**Step 1:** From hint, try
$$P = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 1 \\ 1 & 0 \end{bmatrix}, \quad \det(P) = -1 \neq 0 \;\checkmark, \quad P^{-1} = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 1 \\ 1 & 0 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 2:** Compute $AP$:
$$AP = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 2 \\ 0 & 3 \end{bmatrix}\begin{bmatrix} 0 & 1 \\ 1 & 0 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 0+2 & 1+0 \\ 0+3 & 0+0 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 2 & 1 \\ 3 & 0 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 3:** Compute $P^{-1}AP$:
$$B = P^{-1}AP = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 1 \\ 1 & 0 \end{bmatrix}\begin{bmatrix} 2 & 1 \\ 3 & 0 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 0+3 & 0+0 \\ 2+0 & 1+0 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 3 & 2 \\ 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix} \;\checkmark$$

**Marks Distribution:** Choosing $P$ (2) + Computing $AP$ (2) + Final multiplication (3) = **7 marks**

**Part (b) — 7 marks:**

**Invariant 1 — Determinant:**
$\det(A) = (1)(3) - (2)(0) = 3$
$\det(B) = (3)(1) - (2)(0) = 3$ ✓ **(3.5 marks)**

**Invariant 2 — Trace:**
$\operatorname{tr}(A) = 1 + 3 = 4$
$\operatorname{tr}(B) = 3 + 1 = 4$ ✓ **(3.5 marks)**

**Marks Distribution:** Two invariants computed and matched = **7 marks**

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist (Last-Minute KTU Prep):**

- **Change of basis direction:** Old $\to$ New uses $P^{-1}$; New $\to$ Old uses $P$.
- **Columns of $P$** = new basis vectors expressed in **old** basis coordinates.
- **Similarity:** $B = P^{-1}AP$ (NOT $PAP^{-1}$ unless reverse-relationship is intended).
- **Invertibility check:** Always verify $\det(P) \neq 0$ — non-negotiable on KTU boards.
- **Invariants to memorize (any 3 will secure full marks on invariant questions):**
  1. Determinant
  2. Trace
  3. Eigenvalues
  4. Rank
  5. Characteristic polynomial
  6. Minimal polynomial
  7. Jordan form
- **Diagonalization is a special case of similarity** where $D$ is diagonal.
- **Eigenvalues of triangular $B$** = diagonal entries (exploit this in problems).
- **Cayley-Hamilton link:** Similar matrices share the same minimal polynomial.
- **ML application:** PCA = similarity diagonalization of covariance matrix.
- **Field note:** Over $\mathbb{C}$, every matrix has a Jordan form; over $\mathbb{R}$, orthogonal similarity gives real Jordan form for symmetric matrices.
- **Transpose rule:** $B^T = (P^T)^{-1}A^T P^T$ — useful in dual-space arguments.
- **Power rule:** $A^k$ is similar to $(P^{-1}AP)^k$ via the same $P$.
- **Common KTU trap:** Confusing *change of basis matrix* with *transition matrix for a linear map* — they are dual but distinct concepts.

> **End of Module 2 Notes — Change of Basis and Similarity**
> *Aligned with KTU 2024 Scheme, PEAET753 — Linear Algebra in AI and ML*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Invariant Subspaces
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAET753 - LINEAR ALGEBRA IN AI AND ML
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Invariant Subspaces

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

Let $V$ be a vector space over a field $\mathbb{F}$ and $T : V \to V$ be a linear operator on $V$. A subspace $W \subseteq V$ is called an **Invariant Subspace** under $T$ if for every vector $w \in W$, the image $T(w)$ also lies in $W$.

Mathematically, this is expressed as:

$$T(W) \subseteq W$$

Equivalently, $T$ restricted to $W$ is a linear operator from $W$ to $W$ itself, written as $T|_W : W \to W$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Invariant subspaces are foundational to the structure theorem for linear operators. Every "nice" decomposition of a vector space (Jordan form, Rational Canonical form, Spectral decomposition) is built from a chain or direct sum of invariant subspaces.

### 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Imagine a **revolving door in a hotel lobby**:
- The lobby is the entire vector space $V$.
- The door is the linear operator $T$ (it rotates/transforms people from one position to another).
- An "invariant subspace" is a **cordoned-off VIP section** inside the lobby.
- A person standing in the VIP section, after passing through the revolving door, **always lands back inside the VIP section** — they never get thrown into the general lobby area.

In linear algebra terms: applying the linear transformation $T$ never "escapes" out of the invariant subspace $W$.

### 1.3 Trivial and Universal Invariant Subspaces

For **every** linear operator $T : V \to V$ on a non-zero vector space $V$, the following subspaces are always invariant:

| Subspace | Symbol | Invariance Reason |
|----------|--------|-------------------|
| Zero subspace | $\{0\}$ | $T(0) = 0 \in \{0\}$ |
| Whole space | $V$ | $T(V) \subseteq V$ by definition |
| Kernel of $T$ | $\ker(T)$ | If $Tv=0$, then $T(Tv)=T(0)=0$ |
| Range of $T$ | $\text{Range}(T)$ | $T(\text{Range}(T)) = T(T(V)) \subseteq T(V)$ |
| Eigenspace of $\lambda$ | $E_\lambda$ | $Tv = \lambda v \in E_\lambda$ |

> [!NOTE]
> These are called the **trivial invariant subspaces**. Any other non-trivial invariant subspace carries deep structural information about the operator $T$.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Logical Breakdown of Invariance

**Step 1 — Closure Property Check:**  
A subspace $W$ is $T$-invariant if and only if it is closed under the action of $T$. This means $T$ never "pushes" vectors out of $W$.

**Step 2 — Restriction Operator:**  
Once $W$ is invariant, $T|_W$ becomes a well-defined linear operator on $W$ alone. This restriction inherits all the algebraic properties of $T$ (linearity, characteristic polynomial factors, etc.).

**Step 3 — Matrix Representation:**  
If we choose a basis of $W$ and extend it to a basis of $V$, the matrix of $T$ takes a **block upper-triangular form**:

$$[T]_{\mathcal{B}} = \begin{pmatrix} A & B \\ 0 & C \end{pmatrix}$$

where $A$ represents $T|_W$ in the basis of $W$, and $C$ represents the action on the quotient $V/W$.

**Step 4 — Decomposition Power:**  
If $V = W_1 \oplus W_2 \oplus \cdots \oplus W_k$ where each $W_i$ is $T$-invariant, then $T$ has a **block diagonal matrix** representation, dramatically simplifying computation.

**Step 5 — The "Why" Behind Its Importance:**  
In machine learning, invariant subspaces underpin:
- **Eigendecomposition** of covariance matrices (PCA)
- **Stable feature subspaces** in deep learning (information bottlenecks)
- **Representation learning** where transformations preserve semantic meaning

### 2.2 Key Theorems on Invariant Subspaces

**Theorem 1 (Characteristic Polynomial Restriction):**  
If $W$ is a $T$-invariant subspace of $V$, then the characteristic polynomial of $T|_W$ divides the characteristic polynomial of $T$.

$$\chi_{T|_W}(x) \mid \chi_T(x)$$

**Theorem 2 (Direct Sum Decomposition):**  
If $V = W_1 \oplus W_2$ where both $W_1$ and $W_2$ are $T$-invariant, then:

$$\chi_T(x) = \chi_{T|_{W_1}}(x) \cdot \chi_{T|_{W_2}}(x)$$

**Theorem 3 (Eigenspace Invariance):**  
For any eigenvalue $\lambda$ of $T$, the eigenspace $E_\lambda = \ker(T - \lambda I)$ is a $T$-invariant subspace.

**Theorem 4 (Generalized Eigenspace Invariance):**  
The generalized eigenspace $K_\lambda = \ker(T - \lambda I)^{\dim V}$ is also $T$-invariant.

**Theorem 5 (Primary Decomposition Theorem):**  
If the characteristic polynomial of $T$ factors as $p_1(x)^{r_1} p_2(x)^{r_2} \cdots p_k(x)^{r_k}$ where $p_i$ are distinct irreducible polynomials, then:

$$V = K_{p_1} \oplus K_{p_2} \oplus \cdots \oplus K_{p_k}$$

where each $K_{p_i} = \ker(p_i(T)^{r_i})$ is a $T$-invariant subspace.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Formula / Statement | Key Notes |
|---------|---------------------|-----------|
| Invariance Condition | $T(W) \subseteq W$ | Definition |
| Trivial Invariant Subspaces | $\{0\}$, $V$ | Always invariant |
| Kernel Invariance | $T(\ker T) = \{0\} \subseteq \ker T$ | Always holds |
| Range Invariance | $T(\text{Range}(T)) \subseteq \text{Range}(T)$ | Always holds |
| Eigenspace Invariance | $T(E_\lambda) = \lambda E_\lambda \subseteq E_\lambda$ | Core property |
| Characteristic Poly Divisibility | $\chi_{T\vert_W}(x) \mid \chi_T(x)$ | For invariant $W$ |
| Direct Sum Char. Poly | $\chi_{T} = \prod \chi_{T\vert_{W_i}}$ | When $V = \oplus W_i$ |
| Block Triangular Form | $[T] = \begin{pmatrix} A & B \\\\ 0 & C \end{pmatrix}$ | For invariant $W$ |
| Diagonalizability Criterion | $T$ diagonalizable $\iff$ $V$ is direct sum of 1-D invariant subspaces | KTU favourite |

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Machine Learning (PCA):** The principal component subspaces of a covariance matrix are invariant under the associated symmetric operator.
- **Control Systems:** Controllable and observable subspaces of a linear dynamical system $\dot{x} = Ax + Bu$ are $A$-invariant — fundamental to Kalman decomposition.
- **Quantum Mechanics:** Invariant subspaces correspond to conserved quantities and degeneracies in energy spectra.
- **Network Theory:** Node-connectivity subspaces that remain stable under adjacency matrix transformations.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Block Triangular Form from an Invariant Subspace

**Setup:** Let $V$ be a finite-dimensional vector space with $\dim V = n$, and let $W \subseteq V$ be a $T$-invariant subspace with $\dim W = k$.

**Step 1:** Choose an ordered basis $\mathcal{B}_W = \{w_1, w_2, \ldots, w_k\}$ of $W$.

**Step 2:** Extend $\mathcal{B}_W$ to an ordered basis $\mathcal{B} = \{w_1, \ldots, w_k, v_{k+1}, \ldots, v_n\}$ of $V$.

**Step 3:** Compute the action of $T$ on the basis elements of $W$. Because $T(W) \subseteq W$, every $T(w_i)$ can be written as a linear combination of $\{w_1, \ldots, w_k\}$ alone:

$$T(w_i) = \sum_{j=1}^{k} a_{ji} w_j \quad \text{for } i = 1, 2, \ldots, k$$

This gives us a $k \times k$ matrix $A$ with entries $a_{ji}$.

**Step 4:** For the extended basis vectors $v_{k+1}, \ldots, v_n$, the action of $T$ may produce components both in $W$ and outside $W$:

$$T(v_i) = \sum_{j=1}^{k} b_{ji} w_j + \sum_{j=k+1}^{n} c_{ji} v_j$$

**Step 5:** Writing this in matrix form with respect to $\mathcal{B}$:

$$[T]_{\mathcal{B}} = \begin{pmatrix} A_{(k \times k)} & B_{(k \times (n-k))} \\ 0_{((n-k) \times k)} & C_{((n-k) \times (n-k))} \end{pmatrix}$$

The bottom-left block is **zero** because $T(w_i) \in W$ has no component in the $\{v_{k+1}, \ldots, v_n\}$ directions. This produces the **block upper-triangular** structure.

### 3.2 Worked Example: Verifying Invariance

**Problem:** Let $T : \mathbb{R}^3 \to \mathbb{R}^3$ be defined by $T(x, y, z) = (x + y, y + z, 0)$. Show that $W = \{(a, b, 0) : a, b \in \mathbb{R}\}$ is a $T$-invariant subspace of $\mathbb{R}^3$.

**Step 1 — Identify the subspace $W$:**  
$W$ is the $xy$-plane in $\mathbb{R}^3$, with basis $\{(1, 0, 0), (0, 1, 0)\}$, so $\dim W = 2$.

**Step 2 — Take an arbitrary vector in $W$:**  
Let $w = (a, b, 0) \in W$ for some $a, b \in \mathbb{R}$.

**Step 3 — Apply $T$ to $w$:**

$$\begin{aligned} T(a, b, 0) &= (a + b, \; b + 0, \; 0) \\ &= (a + b, \; b, \; 0) \end{aligned}$$

**Step 4 — Check whether the result lies in $W$:**  
The result $(a + b, b, 0)$ has third coordinate $0$, so it lies in $W$.

**Step 5 — Conclusion:**  
Since $T(w) \in W$ for every $w \in W$, we have $T(W) \subseteq W$, confirming $W$ is $T$-invariant. $\blacksquare$

### 3.3 Worked Example: Matrix Block Decomposition

**Problem:** For the same $T(x, y, z) = (x + y, y + z, 0)$, find the matrix representation of $T$ in the basis $\mathcal{B} = \{(1, 0, 0), (0, 1, 0), (0, 0, 1)\}$ and identify the block structure.

**Step 1 — Compute $T$ on the basis vectors:**

$$\begin{aligned} T(1, 0, 0) &= (1, 0, 0) \\ T(0, 1, 0) &= (1, 1, 0) \\ T(0, 0, 1) &= (0, 1, 0) \end{aligned}$$

**Step 2 — Express results in the basis $\mathcal{B}$:**

$$\begin{aligned} T(1, 0, 0) &= 1 \cdot (1,0,0) + 0 \cdot (0,1,0) + 0 \cdot (0,0,1) \\ T(0, 1, 0) &= 1 \cdot (1,0,0) + 1 \cdot (0,1,0) + 0 \cdot (0,0,1) \\ T(0, 0, 1) &= 0 \cdot (1,0,0) + 1 \cdot (0,1,0) + 0 \cdot (0,0,1) \end{aligned}$$

**Step 3 — Write the matrix:**

$$[T]_{\mathcal{B}} = \begin{pmatrix} 1 & 1 & 0 \\ 0 & 1 & 1 \\ 0 & 0 & 0 \end{pmatrix}$$

**Step 4 — Identify the block structure with $W = \text{span}\{(1,0,0), (0,1,0)\}$:**

$$[T]_{\mathcal{B}} = \begin{pmatrix} A & B \\ 0 & C \end{pmatrix} = \begin{pmatrix} 1 & 1 & \vert & 0 \\ 0 & 1 & \vert & 1 \\ \hline 0 & 0 & \vert & 0 \end{pmatrix}$$

Here, $A = \begin{pmatrix} 1 & 1 \\ 0 & 1 \end{pmatrix}$ represents $T|_W$, $C = (0)$ is the action on $V/W$, and $B = \begin{pmatrix} 0 \\ 1 \end{pmatrix}$.

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Decision Tree for Invariance

| Given Information | Conclusion | Reasoning |
|-------------------|-----------|-----------|
| $w \in W$ arbitrary, then $T(w) \in W$ | $W$ is $T$-invariant | Direct definition check |
| $W$ is a $T$-invariant subspace | $[T]_\mathcal{B}$ is block upper triangular in adapted basis | Block construction theorem |
| $V = W_1 \oplus W_2$, both $T$-invariant | $T$ has block diagonal matrix | Direct consequence |
| $T$ has $k$ distinct eigenvalues in $\mathbb{F}$ | $V$ contains $k$ mutually invariant 1-D subspaces | Eigenspace property |
| $T$ is diagonalizable over $\mathbb{F}$ | $V$ is direct sum of 1-D $T$-invariant subspaces | Spectral theorem |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Conceptual Map: Invariant Subspace Hierarchy

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Vector Space V] --> B[Linear Operator T on V]
    B --> C{Trivial Invariant Subspaces}
    B --> D{Non-trivial Invariant Subspaces}
    C --> C1[Zero Subspace 0]
    C --> C2[Whole Space V]
    C --> C3[Kernel of T]
    C --> C4[Range of T]
    D --> D1[Eigenspace E_lambda]
    D --> D2[Generalized Eigenspace K_lambda]
    D --> D3[Cyclic Subspaces]
    D --> D4[Primary Decomposition Components]
    D1 --> E1[Block Diagonal Matrix]
    D2 --> E2[Jordan Form]
    D3 --> E3[Rational Canonical Form]
    D4 --> E4[Primary Decomposition Theorem]
```

### 4.2 Block Architecture: T-Invariant Subspace Decomposition Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    V[Vector Space V of dim n] --> W1[W1 of dim k1]
    V --> W2[W2 of dim k2]
    V --> W3[Wm of dim km]
    W1 --> A1[Matrix A1 of T restricted to W1]
    W2 --> A2[Matrix A2 of T restricted to W2]
    W3 --> A3[Matrix Am of T restricted to Wm]
    A1 --> BD[Block Diagonal Matrix of T]
    A2 --> BD
    A3 --> BD
    BD --> APP[Application: PCA, Control Systems, Spectral Methods]
```

### 4.3 Verification Flow: Checking if W is T-Invariant

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start[Start: Given T and W] --> Q1{Is W a subspace of V?}
    Q1 -->|No| Out1[Not T-invariant]
    Q1 -->|Yes| Pick[Pick arbitrary w in W]
    Pick --> Apply[Compute T of w]
    Apply --> Q2{Is T of w in W?}
    Q2 -->|No| Out2[Not T-invariant: Counterexample Found]
    Q2 -->|Yes| General[Does this hold for all w in W?]
    General -->|No| Out2
    General -->|Yes| Conclude[W is T-invariant]
    Conclude --> Use[Use Block Triangular Form or Direct Sum]
```

### 4.4 Information Flow: From Invariant Subspaces to Diagonalization

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    IS[Invariant Subspaces Found] --> DSum[V equals W1 direct sum W2 direct sum ... direct sum Wk]
    DSum --> Q{All Wi are 1-Dimensional?}
    Q -->|Yes| Diag[T is Diagonalizable]
    Q -->|No| Q2{All Wi have diagonalizable restriction?}
    Q2 -->|Yes| BlockDiag[T is Block Diagonalizable]
    Q2 -->|No| Jordan[Use Jordan or Rational Canonical Form]
    Diag --> ML1[ML: Eigendecomposition in PCA]
    BlockDiag --> ML2[ML: Stable Subspace Methods]
    Jordan --> ML3[ML: Stability Analysis of Dynamical Systems]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### **PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)**

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**  
**Define an invariant subspace. Give two examples of invariant subspaces of a linear operator $T : V \to V$.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Definition (1.5 Marks):** A subspace $W$ of a vector space $V$ is said to be invariant under a linear operator $T : V \to V$ if $T(W) \subseteq W$, i.e., for every $w \in W$, the image $T(w) \in W$.

**Examples (1.5 Marks):**
1. **The kernel of $T$:** $\ker(T) = \{v \in V : T(v) = 0\}$. If $v \in \ker(T)$, then $T(v) = 0 \in \ker(T)$. Hence $\ker(T)$ is always $T$-invariant.
2. **The range of $T$:** $\text{Range}(T) = \{T(v) : v \in V\}$. If $y = T(v) \in \text{Range}(T)$, then $T(y) = T(T(v)) = T^2(v) \in \text{Range}(T)$. Hence $\text{Range}(T)$ is $T$-invariant.
3. **Eigenspace $E_\lambda$:** For any eigenvalue $\lambda$, $T(E_\lambda) = \lambda E_\lambda \subseteq E_\lambda$.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**  
**State and prove that the eigenspace $E_\lambda$ corresponding to an eigenvalue $\lambda$ of $T$ is a $T$-invariant subspace.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Statement (1 Mark):** For any linear operator $T : V \to V$ and any eigenvalue $\lambda$ of $T$, the eigenspace $E_\lambda = \ker(T - \lambda I)$ is invariant under $T$.

**Proof (2 Marks):**  
Let $v \in E_\lambda$. By definition of eigenspace, $Tv = \lambda v$.

Now consider $T(Tv) = T(\lambda v) = \lambda (Tv) = \lambda (\lambda v) = \lambda^2 v$.

More generally, for any $v \in E_\lambda$:

$$T(v) = \lambda v \in E_\lambda$$

This shows that for every $v \in E_\lambda$, $T(v) \in E_\lambda$. Therefore, $T(E_\lambda) \subseteq E_\lambda$, proving that $E_\lambda$ is a $T$-invariant subspace. $\blacksquare$

---

### **PART B — Module Internal Choice Questions (14 Marks Each)**

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model Paper]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Let $T : \mathbb{R}^3 \to \mathbb{R}^3$ be defined by $T(x, y, z) = (2x + y, x + 2y, z)$. Show that $W_1 = \{(a, b, 0) : a, b \in \mathbb{R}\}$ and $W_2 = \{(0, 0, c) : c \in \mathbb{R}\}$ are both $T$-invariant subspaces. Hence express $T$ in block diagonal form.

**(b) [7 Marks)** Show that if $V = W_1 \oplus W_2$ where $W_1$ and $W_2$ are both $T$-invariant subspaces, then the characteristic polynomial of $T$ satisfies $\chi_T(x) = \chi_{T|_{W_1}}(x) \cdot \chi_{T|_{W_2}}(x)$.

---

### **Model Solution to Question A:**

#### **Part (a) — Solution [7 Marks]:**

**Step 1 — Check $W_1$ is $T$-invariant [2 Marks]:**  
Let $w = (a, b, 0) \in W_1$. Apply $T$:

$$T(a, b, 0) = (2a + b, \; a + 2b, \; 0)$$

The result has third coordinate $0$, so $T(w) \in W_1$. Hence $T(W_1) \subseteq W_1$, and $W_1$ is $T$-invariant.

**Step 2 — Check $W_2$ is $T$-invariant [2 Marks]:**  
Let $w = (0, 0, c) \in W_2$. Apply $T$:

$$T(0, 0, c) = (0 + 0, \; 0 + 0, \; c) = (0, 0, c) \in W_2$$

Hence $T(W_2) \subseteq W_2$, and $W_2$ is $T$-invariant.

**Step 3 — Verify $V = W_1 \oplus W_2$ [1 Mark]:**  
$W_1 \cap W_2 = \{(0, 0, 0)\}$ and $\dim W_1 + \dim W_2 = 2 + 1 = 3 = \dim V$. So $V = W_1 \oplus W_2$.

**Step 4 — Find the matrix of $T$ in the basis $\{(1,0,0), (0,1,0), (0,0,1)\}$ [1 Mark]:**

$$[T] = \begin{pmatrix} 2 & 1 & 0 \\ 1 & 2 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{pmatrix}$$

**Step 5 — Identify the block diagonal form [1 Mark]:**

$$[T] = \begin{pmatrix} 2 & 1 & 0 \\ 1 & 2 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{pmatrix} = \begin{pmatrix} A_{2 \times 2} & 0 \\ 0 & C_{1 \times 1} \end{pmatrix}$$

where $A = \begin{pmatrix} 2 & 1 \\ 1 & 2 \end{pmatrix}$ and $C = (1)$. This confirms the block diagonal decomposition.

#### **Part (b) — Solution [7 Marks]:**

**Step 1 — Set up bases [1 Mark]:**  
Let $\mathcal{B}_1 = \{w_1, \ldots, w_k\}$ be a basis of $W_1$ and $\mathcal{B}_2 = \{w_{k+1}, \ldots, w_n\}$ be a basis of $W_2$. The combined set $\mathcal{B} = \mathcal{B}_1 \cup \mathcal{B}_2$ is a basis of $V$.

**Step 2 — Write the matrix of $T$ in $\mathcal{B}$ [2 Marks]:**  
By invariance, $T(w_i) \in W_1$ for $i = 1, \ldots, k$, and $T(w_j) \in W_2$ for $j = k+1, \ldots, n$. Therefore:

$$[T]_{\mathcal{B}} = \begin{pmatrix} A & 0 \\ 0 & C \end{pmatrix}$$

where $A$ is the matrix of $T|_{W_1}$ and $C$ is the matrix of $T|_{W_2}$.

**Step 3 — Compute the characteristic polynomial [2 Marks]:**  
For a block diagonal matrix, the characteristic polynomial is the product of the characteristic polynomials of the blocks:

$$\chi_T(x) = \det(xI - [T]_{\mathcal{B}}) = \det\begin{pmatrix} xI_k - A & 0 \\ 0 & xI_{n-k} - C \end{pmatrix}$$

**Step 4 — Apply the block determinant rule [2 Marks]:**  
The determinant of a block diagonal matrix is the product of the determinants of the diagonal blocks:

$$\chi_T(x) = \det(xI_k - A) \cdot \det(xI_{n-k} - C) = \chi_{T|_{W_1}}(x) \cdot \chi_{T|_{W_2}}(x)$$

This completes the proof. $\blacksquare$

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model Paper]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Define a $T$-invariant subspace. Prove that $\ker(T)$ and $\text{Range}(T)$ are always $T$-invariant subspaces of $V$.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Consider $T : \mathbb{R}^2 \to \mathbb{R}^2$ defined by $T(x, y) = (2x - y, -x + 2y)$. Find the eigenvalues of $T$ and show that the eigenspaces of $T$ form a direct sum decomposition of $\mathbb{R}^2$ into two $T$-invariant subspaces.

---

### **Model Solution to Question B:**

#### **Part (a) — Solution [7 Marks]:**

**Definition [1 Mark]:** A subspace $W \subseteq V$ is said to be $T$-invariant if $T(W) \subseteq W$, i.e., $T(w) \in W$ for all $w \in W$.

**Proof that $\ker(T)$ is $T$-invariant [3 Marks]:**  
Let $v \in \ker(T)$. By definition, $T(v) = 0$. Now apply $T$ again:

$$T(T(v)) = T(0) = 0$$

So $T(v) \in \ker(T)$. Since this holds for every $v \in \ker(T)$, we conclude $T(\ker(T)) \subseteq \ker(T)$. Hence $\ker(T)$ is $T$-invariant.

**Proof that $\text{Range}(T)$ is $T$-invariant [3 Marks]:**  
Let $y \in \text{Range}(T)$. By definition, there exists some $v \in V$ such that $y = T(v)$. Now:

$$T(y) = T(T(v)) = T^2(v)$$

Since $T^2(v) = T(T(v))$ is itself the image of $T(v)$ under $T$, we have $T(y) \in \text{Range}(T)$. Thus $T(\text{Range}(T)) \subseteq \text{Range}(T)$, proving that $\text{Range}(T)$ is $T$-invariant. $\blacksquare$

#### **Part (b) — Solution [7 Marks]:**

**Step 1 — Find the matrix of $T$ [1 Mark]:**

$$[T] = \begin{pmatrix} 2 & -1 \\ -1 & 2 \end{pmatrix}$$

**Step 2 — Compute the characteristic polynomial [2 Marks]:**

$$\chi_T(x) = \det\begin{pmatrix} x - 2 & 1 \\ 1 & x - 2 \end{pmatrix} = (x-2)^2 - 1 = x^2 - 4x + 3$$

**Step 3 — Solve for eigenvalues [1 Mark]:**

$$x^2 - 4x + 3 = (x-1)(x-3) = 0 \implies x = 1 \text{ or } x = 3$$

**Step 4 — Find the eigenspaces [2 Marks]:**

For $\lambda_1 = 1$:

$$[T - I] = \begin{pmatrix} 1 & -1 \\ -1 & 1 \end{pmatrix} \implies E_1 = \text{span}\{(1, 1)\}$$

For $\lambda_2 = 3$:

$$[T - 3I] = \begin{pmatrix} -1 & -1 \\ -1 & -1 \end{pmatrix} \implies E_3 = \text{span}\{(1, -1)\}$$

**Step 5 — Verify direct sum decomposition [1 Mark]:**  
The vectors $(1, 1)$ and $(1, -1)$ are linearly independent (neither is a scalar multiple of the other), so:

$$\mathbb{R}^2 = E_1 \oplus E_3 = \text{span}\{(1,1)\} \oplus \text{span}\{(1,-1)\}$$

By the eigenspace invariance theorem, both $E_1$ and $E_3$ are $T$-invariant subspaces, completing the decomposition. $\blacksquare$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout**
> 1. **Confusing "$T(w) \in W$" with "$T(w) = w$"**: Invariance does NOT mean fixed points — it means the image stays in $W$. Students often lose 1-2 marks by writing "T(w) = w" instead of "T(w) ∈ W".
> 2. **Forgetting to verify $W$ is a subspace first**: Some students jump to checking $T(W) \subseteq W$ without first confirming that $W$ is closed under addition and scalar multiplication. Always verify subspace axioms when $W$ is not explicitly given as a span.
> 3. **Missing the block-diagonal form**: When a question says "decompose", students must explicitly write the block diagonal matrix, not just state the subspaces. Failing to write the matrix costs 2-3 marks.
> 4. **Confusing characteristic polynomial formulas**: $\chi_{T|_W}(x)$ divides $\chi_T(x)$ — not the other way around. Reversing this direction is a common error.
> 5. **In direct sum proofs**: You must show BOTH that $W_1 \cap W_2 = \{0\}$ AND that $W_1 + W_2 = V$ (or use dimension count). Showing only one condition costs 1 mark.

---

### **Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember**

- **Core Definition:** $W \subseteq V$ is $T$-invariant if $T(W) \subseteq W$ (equivalently, $T|_W$ is a self-map of $W$).
- **Always Invariant:** $\{0\}$, $V$, $\ker(T)$, $\text{Range}(T)$, every eigenspace $E_\lambda$, every generalized eigenspace $K_\lambda$.
- **Block Structure:** Invariance gives block upper-triangular matrices; direct sum of invariant subspaces gives block diagonal matrices.
- **Characteristic Polynomial Property:** $\chi_{T|_W}(x) \mid \chi_T(x)$ for any invariant $W$.
- **Diagonalization Equivalence:** $T$ is diagonalizable $\iff$ $V$ is a direct sum of 1-D $T$-invariant subspaces.
- **Primary Decomposition:** Distinct irreducible factors of $\chi_T$ yield $T$-invariant primary components.
- **Key Test Trick:** Pick arbitrary $w \in W$, compute $T(w)$, and check membership in $W$.
- **Matrix Test Trick:** In an adapted basis, the bottom-left block must be zero for $W$ to be invariant.
- **Engineering Relevance:** PCA, control theory, quantum mechanics, dynamical systems, representation learning.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Norms, Inner Products, and Orthogonality – Vector Norms
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAET753 - LINEAR ALGEBRA IN AI AND ML
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Norms, Inner Products, and Orthogonality – Vector Norms

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition of a Vector Norm

A **vector norm** is a function that assigns a strictly non-negative real number (a magnitude or "length") to every vector in a vector space, satisfying a specific set of axioms that generalize our intuitive notion of distance from the origin.

Formally, a function $\|\cdot\| : \mathbb{R}^n \to \mathbb{R}$ is called a **norm** on $\mathbb{R}^n$ if for all vectors $\mathbf{x}, \mathbf{y} \in \mathbb{R}^n$ and for all scalars $\alpha \in \mathbb{R}$, the following three axioms hold:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{(N1) Non-negativity:} \quad & \|\mathbf{x}\| \ge 0, \text{ and } \|\mathbf{x}\| = 0 \iff \mathbf{x} = \mathbf{0} \\
\text{(N2) Homogeneity (Absolute Homogeneity):} \quad & \|\alpha \mathbf{x}\| = \vert \alpha \vert \, \|\mathbf{x}\| \\
\text{(N3) Triangle Inequality:} \quad & \|\mathbf{x} + \mathbf{y}\| \le \|\mathbf{x}\| + \|\mathbf{y}\|
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Note:** A vector norm is **not unique**. The same vector can have many different "lengths" depending on which norm we choose. The Euclidean length is only *one* of infinitely many valid measures of magnitude.

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Imagine you are standing in a city grid (like Manhattan). To walk from point $A$ to point $B$, you cannot cut diagonally through buildings — you must walk along the horizontal avenues and vertical streets. The total blocks you walk is the **Manhattan distance** (L1 norm).

Now imagine you are a bird flying in the open sky from the same point $A$ to point $B$. You can travel in a perfectly straight line. The length of that straight line is the **Euclidean distance** (L2 norm).

Finally, imagine you are a chess king that can only move one square at a time in any direction. The number of moves it takes to reach $B$ from $A$ is the **Chebyshev distance** (L∞ norm).

> **Key Intuition:** A norm is simply a *recipe* for measuring how "big" a vector is. Different recipes give different answers, and each recipe is useful in a different context. In AI/ML, the choice of norm determines how a model measures "error" or "similarity" between data points.

## 1.3 The Three Most Common Norms (Quick Preview)

For a vector $\mathbf{x} = (x_1, x_2, \dots, x_n) \in \mathbb{R}^n$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{L1 Norm (Manhattan):} \quad & \|\mathbf{x}\|_1 = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \vert x_i \vert \\
\text{L2 Norm (Euclidean):} \quad & \|\mathbf{x}\|_2 = \sqrt{\sum_{i=1}^{n} x_i^2} \\
\text{L}\infty \text{ Norm (Max/Chebyshev):} \quad & \|\mathbf{x}\|_\infty = \max_{1 \le i \le n} \vert x_i \vert
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Notation Warning:** The norm symbols $\| \cdot \|$ should never be confused with absolute value signs $\vert \cdot \vert$. While a norm maps a *vector* to a *scalar*, an absolute value maps a *scalar* to a *scalar*. In $\mathbb{R}^1$ they happen to coincide, but in $\mathbb{R}^n$ for $n \ge 2$, the norm is a fundamentally distinct object.

## 1.4 Why Norms Matter in AI and ML

| AI/ML Application | Role of the Norm |
|-------------------|------------------|
| **Regularization (Lasso, Ridge)** | Penalizes large weights using L1 / L2 norms |
| **K-Nearest Neighbors (KNN)** | Measures distance between data points |
| **Gradient Descent Convergence** | Norm of gradient measures step size |
| **Word Embeddings (Word2Vec, GloVe)** | Cosine similarity uses L2 norm |
| **Loss Functions (MSE, MAE)** | Built directly from L2 and L1 norms |
| **Adversarial Robustness** | L∞ norm bounds perturbation magnitude |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Detailed Analysis of the Three Axioms

Let us dissect what each axiom really *means* geometrically.

### Axiom (N1): Non-negativity & Definiteness
- A norm is *always* a non-negative real number. You cannot have a "negative length."
- The norm equals **zero if and only if** the vector is the zero vector $\mathbf{0}$. This is a strict equivalence ($\iff$), meaning:
  - If $\mathbf{x} = \mathbf{0}$, then $\|\mathbf{x}\| = 0$ (no length for no vector).
  - If $\|\mathbf{x}\| = 0$, then $\mathbf{x}$ must be $\mathbf{0}$ (no other vector can have zero length).

### Axiom (N2): Absolute Homogeneity (Scaling Property)
- Scaling a vector by a scalar $\alpha$ scales its norm by $\vert \alpha \vert$.
- Geometrically: if you double the vector, you double its length. If you flip its direction (negative scalar), the length is preserved.
- This is why $\|-3 \mathbf{x}\| = 3\|\mathbf{x}\|$, *not* $-3\|\mathbf{x}\|$. The absolute value ensures non-negativity.

### Axiom (N3): Triangle Inequality
- The shortest path between two points is a straight line.
- $\|\mathbf{x} + \mathbf{y}\|$ is the direct distance from origin to $\mathbf{x} + \mathbf{y}$.
- $\|\mathbf{x}\| + \|\mathbf{y}\|$ is the distance obtained by going through $\mathbf{x}$ first, then to $\mathbf{x} + \mathbf{y}$.
- The inequality says: *direct path $\le$ indirect path*. This is the **law of the triangle**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Geometric Corollary (Reverse Triangle Inequality):** From N3 we can derive $\|\mathbf{x} - \mathbf{y}\| \ge \big\vert \|\mathbf{x}\| - \|\mathbf{y}\| \big\vert$. This is essential in machine learning theory to bound differences in model outputs.

## 2.2 The General p-Norm (Holder Norm Family)

The three common norms are special cases of a single family called the **$L^p$ norm** (or $p$-norm), defined for any real number $p \ge 1$ as:

$$
\|\mathbf{x}\|_p = \left( \sum_{i=1}^{n} \vert x_i \vert^p \right)^{1/p}
$$

The value of $p$ acts as a *dial* that smoothly transitions between the three cases:

- As $p \to 1^+$, the $p$-norm approaches the L1 norm.
- At $p = 2$, we get the standard Euclidean norm.
- As $p \to \infty$, the $p$-norm approaches the max-norm $\|\mathbf{x}\|_\infty$.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examiner Insight:** A very common examination trick is to test whether you know that $p \ge 1$ is *required* for a valid norm. For $0 < p < 1$, the function $\left( \sum \vert x_i \vert^p \right)^{1/p}$ is *not* a norm because it violates the triangle inequality — it is called a **quasi-norm**.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CRITICAL TABLE FORMATTING RULE:** The vertical pipe symbol `|` is used as a column separator in markdown tables. Since our LaTeX expressions contain absolute values, we have replaced `|` with the dedicated LaTeX command `\vert` to avoid breaking the table structure. This is the official KTU notation standard.

| **Norm** | **Symbol** | **Formula for $\mathbf{x} = (x_1, \dots, x_n)$** | **Geometric Shape (Unit Ball in $\mathbb{R}^2$)** | **Key Use in ML** |
|----------|-----------|--------------------------------------------------|--------------------------------------------------|-------------------|
| L0 (Quasi) | $\|\mathbf{x}\|_0$ | $\sum_{i=1}^{n} \mathbb{1}[x_i \ne 0]$ | Not a true norm | Feature sparsity count |
| L1 (Manhattan) | $\|\mathbf{x}\|_1$ | $\sum_{i=1}^{n} \vert x_i \vert$ | Diamond (rotated square) | Lasso regression, sparse features |
| L2 (Euclidean) | $\|\mathbf{x}\|_2$ | $\left( \sum_{i=1}^{n} x_i^2 \right)^{1/2}$ | Circle | Ridge regression, KNN, PCA |
| Lp (General) | $\|\mathbf{x}\|_p$ | $\left( \sum_{i=1}^{n} \vert x_i \vert^p \right)^{1/p}$ | Smooth convex curve | Theoretical analysis |
| L∞ (Max) | $\|\mathbf{x}\|_\infty$ | $\max_{1 \le i \le n} \vert x_i \vert$ | Square (axis-aligned) | Adversarial robustness, clipping |
| L−∞ (Min) | $\|\mathbf{x}\|_{-\infty}$ | $\min_{1 \le i \le n} \vert x_i \vert$ | Not commonly used | Rare theoretical bounds |

## 2.4 Unit Vectors and the Unit Ball

A **unit vector** is any vector whose norm equals $1$. The **unit ball** in norm $\|\cdot\|$ is the set of all unit vectors:

$$
B = \{\mathbf{x} \in \mathbb{R}^n : \|\mathbf{x}\| \le 1\}
$$

The *shape* of the unit ball depends entirely on the norm:
- For L2, the unit ball is a sphere (circle in 2D).
- For L1, the unit ball is a diamond (square rotated 45°).
- For L∞, the unit ball is an axis-aligned square.

> [!NOTE]
> **Nested Norms Inequality (Extremely High-Yield for KTU):** For any vector $\mathbf{x} \in \mathbb{R}^n$ and any $1 \le p < q \le \infty$:
> $$\|\mathbf{x}\|_q \le \|\mathbf{x}\|_p \le n^{\frac{1}{p} - \frac{1}{q}} \, \|\mathbf{x}\|_q$$
> In particular, $\|\mathbf{x}\|_\infty \le \|\mathbf{x}\|_2 \le \|\mathbf{x}\|_1 \le \sqrt{n} \, \|\mathbf{x}\|_2 \le n \, \|\mathbf{x}\|_\infty$. This inequality is asked frequently in KTU exams.

## 2.5 Equivalence of Norms (Finite-Dimensional Result)

In $\mathbb{R}^n$, all norms are *equivalent*: there exist positive constants $c, C > 0$ such that for all $\mathbf{x} \in \mathbb{R}^n$:

$$
c \, \|\mathbf{x}\|_a \le \|\mathbf{x}\|_b \le C \, \|\mathbf{x}\|_a
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This equivalence is what guarantees that **convergence in one norm implies convergence in any other norm** in finite-dimensional spaces. This is why the choice of norm does not affect the limit of a convergent sequence in $\mathbb{R}^n$ — only the *rate* of convergence and the geometric interpretation differ.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example 1: Computing All Common Norms for a Vector

**Problem:** Compute $\|\mathbf{x}\|_1$, $\|\mathbf{x}\|_2$, and $\|\mathbf{x}\|_\infty$ for $\mathbf{x} = (3, -4, 2) \in \mathbb{R}^3$.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1: Compute the L1 norm (Manhattan / Taxicab norm).**

The L1 norm is the sum of absolute values of all components.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\|\mathbf{x}\|_1 &= \sum_{i=1}^{3} \vert x_i \vert \\
&= \vert 3 \vert + \vert -4 \vert + \vert 2 \vert \\
&= 3 + 4 + 2 \\
&= 9
\end{aligned}
$$

The L1 norm is **9**. The absolute value $\vert -4 \vert = 4$ is taken because distance is always non-negative.

**Step 2: Compute the L2 norm (Euclidean norm).**

The L2 norm is the square root of the sum of squared components.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\|\mathbf{x}\|_2 &= \sqrt{\sum_{i=1}^{3} x_i^2} \\
&= \sqrt{3^2 + (-4)^2 + 2^2} \\
&= \sqrt{9 + 16 + 4} \\
&= \sqrt{29} \\
&\approx 5.385
\end{aligned}
$$

Note that the L2 norm squares each component, which **eliminates the sign** (since $(-4)^2 = 16$). The L2 norm is **$\sqrt{29}$**.

**Step 3: Compute the L∞ norm (Max norm / Chebyshev norm).**

The L∞ norm is the largest absolute component of the vector.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\|\mathbf{x}\|_\infty &= \max_{1 \le i \le 3} \vert x_i \vert \\
&= \max\left( \vert 3 \vert, \vert -4 \vert, \vert 2 \vert \right) \\
&= \max(3, 4, 2) \\
&= 4
\end{aligned}
$$

The L∞ norm is **4**, corresponding to the second component $x_2 = -4$.

**Step 4: Verify the Nested Norms Inequality.**

We verify the theoretical bound $\|\mathbf{x}\|_\infty \le \|\mathbf{x}\|_2 \le \|\mathbf{x}\|_1$:

$$
4 \le \sqrt{29} \approx 5.385 \le 9
$$

All three inequalities hold, confirming our computation is consistent.

---

## 3.2 Worked Example 2: Verifying the Triangle Inequality for L2 Norm

**Problem:** Prove that the L2 norm satisfies the triangle inequality for any $\mathbf{x}, \mathbf{y} \in \mathbb{R}^n$:
$$\|\mathbf{x} + \mathbf{y}\|_2 \le \|\mathbf{x}\|_2 + \|\mathbf{y}\|_2$$

### Step-by-Step Proof

**Step 1: Expand the squared norm of the sum.**

We start with the square of the L2 norm of the sum, because squaring eliminates the troublesome square root.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\|\mathbf{x} + \mathbf{y}\|_2^2 &= (\mathbf{x} + \mathbf{y}) \cdot (\mathbf{x} + \mathbf{y}) \\
&= \mathbf{x} \cdot \mathbf{x} + 2 \, \mathbf{x} \cdot \mathbf{y} + \mathbf{y} \cdot \mathbf{y} \\
&= \|\mathbf{x}\|_2^2 + 2 \, \mathbf{x} \cdot \mathbf{y} + \|\mathbf{y}\|_2^2
\end{aligned}
$$

This expansion uses the distributive property of the dot product.

**Step 2: Apply the Cauchy–Schwarz inequality.**

The Cauchy–Schwarz inequality (which is a theorem in inner product spaces) states that for any vectors $\mathbf{x}, \mathbf{y}$:

$$
\mathbf{x} \cdot \mathbf{y} \le \vert \mathbf{x} \cdot \mathbf{y} \vert \le \|\mathbf{x}\|_2 \, \|\mathbf{y}\|_2
$$

Applying this upper bound to the cross term $2 \, \mathbf{x} \cdot \mathbf{y} \le 2 \, \|\mathbf{x}\|_2 \, \|\mathbf{y}\|_2$, we obtain:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\|\mathbf{x} + \mathbf{y}\|_2^2 &\le \|\mathbf{x}\|_2^2 + 2 \, \|\mathbf{x}\|_2 \, \|\mathbf{y}\|_2 + \|\mathbf{y}\|_2^2 \\
&= \left( \|\mathbf{x}\|_2 + \|\mathbf{y}\|_2 \right)^2
\end{aligned}
$$

The right-hand side is a perfect square of a non-negative real number.

**Step 3: Take the square root of both sides.**

Since the L2 norm is non-negative, taking the square root preserves the inequality:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sqrt{\|\mathbf{x} + \mathbf{y}\|_2^2} &\le \sqrt{\left( \|\mathbf{x}\|_2 + \|\mathbf{y}\|_2 \right)^2} \\
\|\mathbf{x} + \mathbf{y}\|_2 &\le \|\mathbf{x}\|_2 + \|\mathbf{y}\|_2
\end{aligned}
$$

This completes the proof. $\blacksquare$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Examination Note:** The Cauchy–Schwarz inequality is the *engine* that drives almost every norm inequality. KTU examiners expect you to either cite it directly or use a coordinate-level argument to justify the cross-term bound.

---

## 3.3 Worked Example 3: Proving the L∞ Norm Satisfies All Three Axioms

**Problem:** Prove that $\|\mathbf{x}\|_\infty = \max_i \vert x_i \vert$ is a valid norm on $\mathbb{R}^n$.

### Step-by-Step Proof

**Step 1: Prove Non-negativity and Definiteness (N1).**

By definition of the maximum of absolute values:
- $\vert x_i \vert \ge 0$ for every $i$, so the maximum is non-negative: $\|\mathbf{x}\|_\infty \ge 0$.
- $\|\mathbf{x}\|_\infty = 0$ implies $\max_i \vert x_i \vert = 0$, which implies $\vert x_i \vert = 0$ for all $i$, which implies $x_i = 0$ for all $i$, so $\mathbf{x} = \mathbf{0}$.
- Conversely, if $\mathbf{x} = \mathbf{0}$, all $\vert x_i \vert = 0$, so the maximum is $0$.

**Step 2: Prove Absolute Homogeneity (N2).**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\|\alpha \mathbf{x}\|_\infty &= \max_{1 \le i \le n} \vert \alpha x_i \vert \\
&= \max_{1 \le i \le n} \left( \vert \alpha \vert \cdot \vert x_i \vert \right) \\
&= \vert \alpha \vert \cdot \max_{1 \le i \le n} \vert x_i \vert \\
&= \vert \alpha \vert \, \|\mathbf{x}\|_\infty
\end{aligned}
$$

We used the property $\vert \alpha x_i \vert = \vert \alpha \vert \vert x_i \vert$ and the fact that a positive scalar $\vert \alpha \vert$ can be factored out of a maximum.

**Step 3: Prove the Triangle Inequality (N3).**

Let $i^*$ be the index where the maximum is achieved for $\mathbf{x} + \mathbf{y}$:
$$\|\mathbf{x} + \mathbf{y}\|_\infty = \vert x_{i^*} + y_{i^*} \vert$$

By the scalar triangle inequality:
$$\vert x_{i^*} + y_{i^*} \vert \le \vert x_{i^*} \vert + \vert y_{i^*} \vert$$

Now, since $\vert x_{i^*} \vert \le \max_i \vert x_i \vert = \|\mathbf{x}\|_\infty$ and similarly for $y$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\|\mathbf{x} + \mathbf{y}\|_\infty &\le \|\mathbf{x}\|_\infty + \|\mathbf{y}\|_\infty
\end{aligned}
$$

This completes the proof. $\blacksquare$

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram: Norm Hierarchy and Relationships

This diagram visualizes the family of $L^p$ norms as a continuum, with the three classical norms as specific points on it. The shape of the unit ball in $\mathbb{R}^2$ morphs as $p$ changes.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["Lp Norm Family Definition"] --> B["General p Norm"]
    A --> C["Limit as p to 1"]
    A --> D["Limit as p to infinity"]

    B --> B1["p equals 1: L1 Manhattan Norm"]
    B --> B2["p equals 2: L2 Euclidean Norm"]
    B --> B3["p equals infinity: L Infinity Max Norm"]

    C --> C1["Shape: Diamond in R2"]
    D --> D1["Shape: Square in R2"]

    B1 --> E1["Use Case: Lasso, Sparsity"]
    B2 --> E2["Use Case: Ridge, KNN, PCA"]
    B3 --> E3["Use Case: Adversarial Robustness"]

    B2 --> F["Cauchy Schwarz Bridge"]
    F --> B1
    F --> B3

    G["Input Vector x in Rn"] --> H["Apply Norm Function"]
    H --> I1["Output: Scalar Length Value"]

    I1 --> J1["Non Negativity Check"]
    I1 --> J2["Homogeneity Check"]
    I1 --> J3["Triangle Inequality Check"]
    J1 --> K["Valid Norm Certified"]
    J2 --> K
    J3 --> K
```

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Norm in an ML Pipeline

This schematic shows where the vector norm appears in a typical supervised learning pipeline, mapping the abstract mathematical object to a concrete functional role.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph INPUT["Data Input Layer"]
        X["Feature Vector x of dimension n"]
        Y["Label or Prediction y of dimension n"]
    end

    subgraph COMPUTE["Norm Computation Engine"]
        N1["Compute L1 Norm: sum of absolute values"]
        N2["Compute L2 Norm: square root of sum of squares"]
        N3["Compute L Infinity Norm: maximum absolute value"]
    end

    subgraph MLAPP["Machine Learning Application Layer"]
        APP1["Regression Loss Function: MSE, MAE"]
        APP2["Regularization Penalty: Lasso L1, Ridge L2"]
        APP3["Distance Metric for KNN and Clustering"]
        APP4["Gradient Magnitude for Convergence Check"]
    end

    subgraph OUTPUT["Output Decision Layer"]
        OUT1["Scalar Loss Value for Backpropagation"]
        OUT2["Sparsity Inducing Weight Update"]
        OUT3["Nearest Neighbor Class Label"]
        OUT4["Convergence Flag: Stop or Continue"]
    end

    X --> N1
    X --> N2
    X --> N3
    Y --> N1
    Y --> N2
    Y --> N3

    N1 --> APP1
    N2 --> APP1
    N1 --> APP2
    N2 --> APP2
    N1 --> APP3
    N2 --> APP3
    N3 --> APP3
    N2 --> APP4
    N3 --> APP4

    APP1 --> OUT1
    APP2 --> OUT2
    APP3 --> OUT3
    APP4 --> OUT4
```

## 4.3 Conceptual Diagram: Unit Balls in $\mathbb{R}^2$ for Different Norms

This diagram (rendered as a schematic) illustrates how the *shape* of the unit ball $\{(x_1, x_2) : \|\cdot\| = 1\}$ changes with the choice of $p$. The boundary is plotted by solving $\vert x_1 \vert^p + \vert x_2 \vert^p = 1$.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1["p equals 0.5: Astroid shape, concave, NOT a norm"] --> NOTE1["Quasi norm: violates triangle inequality"]
    P2["p equals 1: Diamond with vertices at 1,0 minus 1,0 0,1 0,minus 1"] --> NOTE2["L1 norm: aligned with axes, encourages sparsity"]
    P3["p equals 2: Smooth circle of radius 1"] --> NOTE3["L2 norm: rotation invariant, geometrically natural"]
    P4["p equals 4: Rounded square, slightly inside circle"] --> NOTE4["Intermediate shape, approaches square as p increases"]
    P5["p equals infinity: Axis aligned square with vertices at 1,1 minus 1,1 1,minus 1 minus 1,minus 1"] --> NOTE5["L infinity norm: dominated by largest component"]

    P1 --> P2 --> P3 --> P4 --> P5

    ARROW["As p increases, the unit ball morphs from diamond to circle to square"]:::note
    P2 -.-> ARROW
    P3 -.-> ARROW
    P5 -.-> ARROW

    classDef note fill:#f9f,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagram:** The vertices of each unit ball are the "extreme" directions. For L1, the vertices lie on the axes (encouraging sparse solutions — only one coordinate is nonzero). For L∞, the vertices lie on the diagonals (one coordinate dominates). For L2, the ball is a circle (no coordinate preference). This geometric intuition is the *deep reason* why L1 regularization produces sparse weights in machine learning.
