# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Proof that $A = U\Sigma V^{T}$ Satisfies the Required Identities

We will verify that the construction logically yields the SVD identities used in all KTU problems.

**Setup:** Let $A \in \mathbb{R}^{m \times n}$ have rank $r$. Form $A^{T}A \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times n}$, a symmetric positive semi-definite matrix. By the spectral theorem:

$$A^{T}A v_i = \lambda_i v_i, \quad i = 1, 2, \dots, n$$

with $v_i$ orthonormal ($V^{T}V = I_n$) and $\lambda_1 \geq \lambda_2 \geq \dots \geq \lambda_n \geq 0$.

**Derive the form $A^{T}A = V \Sigma^{T}\Sigma V^{T}$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
A^{T}A &= A^{T}A \cdot I_n \\
&= A^{T}A \cdot (V V^{T}) && \text{(since } V V^{T} = I_n \text{ for orthogonal } V) \\
&= A^{T}A \sum_{i=1}^{n} v_i v_i^{T} \\
&= \sum_{i=1}^{n} (A^{T}A v_i) v_i^{T} \\
&= \sum_{i=1}^{n} (\lambda_i v_i) v_i^{T} \\
&= \sum_{i=1}^{n} v_i \lambda_i v_i^{T} \\
&= V \Lambda V^{T} && \text{(writing } \Lambda = \text{diag}(\lambda_1, \dots, \lambda_n))
\end{aligned}
$$

Setting $\sigma_i = \sqrt{\lambda_i}$ (with $\sigma_i \geq 0$), and defining $\Sigma^{T}\Sigma = \text{diag}(\sigma_1^{2}, \dots, \sigma_n^{2}) = \Lambda$, we get:

$$A^{T}A = V \Sigma^{T}\Sigma V^{T} = V \Sigma^{T} \Sigma V^{T}$$

**Construct $U$:** For $i = 1, \dots, r$, where $\sigma_i > 0$, define:

$$u_i = \frac{1}{\sigma_i} A v_i$$

**Verify orthonormality of $u_i$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
u_i^{T} u_j &= \left(\frac{1}{\sigma_i} A v_i\right)^{T} \left(\frac{1}{\sigma_j} A v_j\right) \\
&= \frac{1}{\sigma_i \sigma_j} v_i^{T} A^{T} A v_j \\
&= \frac{1}{\sigma_i \sigma_j} v_i^{T} (\sigma_j^{2} v_j) && \text{(since } A^{T}A v_j = \sigma_j^{2} v_j) \\
&= \frac{\sigma_j}{\sigma_i} v_i^{T} v_j \\
&= \frac{\sigma_j}{\sigma_i} \delta_{ij}
\end{aligned}
$$

For $i = j$: $u_i^{T} u_i = 1$. For $i \neq j$: $u_i^{T} u_j = 0$. So $\{u_1, \dots, u_r\}$ is orthonormal.

**Verify the main identity $A v_i = \sigma_i u_i$:**

By construction, $u_i = \frac{1}{\sigma_i} A v_i$ implies $A v_i = \sigma_i u_i$. ✓

**Extend $U$ to $m \times m$:** If $m > r$, add any $m - r$ orthonormal vectors completing the basis of $\mathbb{R}^{m}$.

**Verify $A = U \Sigma V^{T}$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
U \Sigma V^{T} &= U \Sigma \sum_{i=1}^{n} v_i e_i^{T} \\
&= \sum_{i=1}^{n} (U \Sigma e_i) v_i^{T} \\
&= \sum_{i=1}^{n} (\sigma_i u_i) v_i^{T} && \text{(the } i\text{-th column of } U\Sigma) \\
&= \sum_{i=1}^{r} \sigma_i u_i v_i^{T} + \sum_{i=r+1}^{n} 0 \\
&= \sum_{i=1}^{r} A v_i v_i^{T} && \text{(using } \sigma_i u_i = A v_i) \\
&= A \sum_{i=1}^{r} v_i v_i^{T} \\
&= A V_r V_r^{T} \\
&= A \cdot I_n && \text{(projection onto column space of } V) \\
&= A && \blacksquare
\end{aligned}
$$

---

## 3.2 Worked Example: Full SVD of a $2 \times 2$ Matrix

> [!NOTE] > **KTU 2024 Style:** The following is a complete, board-ready worked problem typical of a 14-mark ESE question.

**Problem:** Find the Singular Value Decomposition of:
$$A = \begin{pmatrix} 1 & 2 \\ 2 & 1 \end{pmatrix}$$

### Step 1 — Compute $A^{T}A$

$$
A^{T}A = \begin{pmatrix} 1 & 2 \\ 2 & 1 \end{pmatrix}^{T} \begin{pmatrix} 1 & 2 \\ 2 & 1 \end{pmatrix} = \begin{pmatrix} 1 & 2 \\ 2 & 1 \end{pmatrix} \begin{pmatrix} 1 & 2 \\ 2 & 1 \end{pmatrix}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
A^{T}A &= \begin{pmatrix} 1\cdot 1 + 2\cdot 2 & 1\cdot 2 + 2\cdot 1 \\ 2\cdot 1 + 1\cdot 2 & 2\cdot 2 + 1\cdot 1 \end{pmatrix} \\
&= \begin{pmatrix} 5 & 4 \\ 4 & 5 \end{pmatrix}
\end{aligned}
$$

Note: $A$ is symmetric, so $A^{T}A = A^{2}$.

### Step 2 — Find Eigenvalues of $A^{T}A$

Characteristic equation:
$$\det(A^{T}A - \lambda I) = 0 \implies (5 - \lambda)^{2} - 16 = 0$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
(5 - \lambda)^{2} &= 16 \\
5 - \lambda &= \pm 4 \\
\lambda_1 &= 9, \quad \lambda_2 = 1
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 3 — Compute Singular Values

$$\sigma_1 = \sqrt{\lambda_1} = \sqrt{9} = 3, \quad \sigma_2 = \sqrt{\lambda_2} = \sqrt{1} = 1$$

### Step 4 — Find Right Singular Vectors (Eigenvectors of $A^{T}A$)

**For $\lambda_1 = 9$:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
(A^{T}A - 9I) v_1 &= 0 \\
\begin{pmatrix} -4 & 4 \\ 4 & -4 \end{pmatrix} v_1 &= 0 \\
\Rightarrow v_1 &= \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}} \begin{pmatrix} 1 \\ 1 \end{pmatrix}
\end{aligned}
$$

**For $\lambda_2 = 1$:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
(A^{T}A - I) v_2 &= 0 \\
\begin{pmatrix} 4 & 4 \\ 4 & 4 \end{pmatrix} v_2 &= 0 \\
\Rightarrow v_2 &= \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}} \begin{pmatrix} 1 \\ -1 \end{pmatrix}
\end{aligned}
$$

Therefore:
$$V = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}} \begin{pmatrix} 1 & 1 \\ 1 & -1 \end{pmatrix}$$

### Step 5 — Construct $\Sigma$

Since $A$ is $2 \times 2$ and full rank, $\Sigma$ is $2 \times 2$:

$$\Sigma = \begin{pmatrix} 3 & 0 \\ 0 & 1 \end{pmatrix}$$

### Step 6 — Compute Left Singular Vectors

**For $u_1$:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
u_1 &= \frac{1}{\sigma_1} A v_1 = \frac{1}{3} \begin{pmatrix} 1 & 2 \\ 2 & 1 \end{pmatrix} \cdot \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}} \begin{pmatrix} 1 \\ 1 \end{pmatrix} \\
&= \frac{1}{3\sqrt{2}} \begin{pmatrix} 1 + 2 \\ 2 + 1 \end{pmatrix} = \frac{1}{3\sqrt{2}} \begin{pmatrix} 3 \\ 3 \end{pmatrix} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}} \begin{pmatrix} 1 \\ 1 \end{pmatrix}
\end{aligned}
$$

**For $u_2$:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
u_2 &= \frac{1}{\sigma_2} A v_2 = \frac{1}{1} \begin{pmatrix} 1 & 2 \\ 2 & 1 \end{pmatrix} \cdot \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}} \begin{pmatrix} 1 \\ -1 \end{pmatrix} \\
&= \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}} \begin{pmatrix} 1 - 2 \\ 2 - 1 \end{pmatrix} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}} \begin{pmatrix} -1 \\ 1 \end{pmatrix}
\end{aligned}
$$

Therefore:
$$U = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}} \begin{pmatrix} 1 & -1 \\ 1 & 1 \end{pmatrix}$$

### Step 7 — Verify the Decomposition

$$
\begin{aligned}
U \Sigma V^{T} &= \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}} \begin{pmatrix} 1 & -1 \\ 1 & 1 \end{pmatrix} \begin{pmatrix} 3 & 0 \\ 0 & 1 \end{pmatrix} \cdot \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}} \begin{pmatrix} 1 & 1 \\ 1 & -1 \end{pmatrix} \\
&= \frac{1}{2} \begin{pmatrix} 3 & -1 \\ 3 & 1 \end{pmatrix} \begin{pmatrix} 1 & 1 \\ 1 & -1 \end{pmatrix} \\
&= \frac{1}{2} \begin{pmatrix} 3 - 1 & 3 + 1 \\ 3 + 1 & 3 - 1 \end{pmatrix} = \frac{1}{2} \begin{pmatrix} 2 & 4 \\ 4 & 2 \end{pmatrix} = \begin{pmatrix} 1 & 2 \\ 2 & 1 \end{pmatrix} = A \quad \checkmark
\end{aligned}
$$

### Final SVD Result

$$
\boxed{
A = \underbrace{\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}} \begin{pmatrix} 1 & -1 \\ 1 & 1 \end{pmatrix}}_{U} \cdot \underbrace{\begin{pmatrix} 3 & 0 \\ 0 & 1 \end{pmatrix}}_{\Sigma} \cdot \underbrace{\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}} \begin{pmatrix} 1 & 1 \\ 1 & -1 \end{pmatrix}}_{V^{T}}
}
$$

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Best Rank-$k$ Approximation (Eckart–Young Theorem)

> [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 High-Yield Result:** The SVD provides the *provably optimal* low-rank approximation.

**Theorem (Eckart–Young, 1936):** For any $A \in \mathbb{R}^{m \times n}$ with SVD $A = U\Sigma V^{T}$, the matrix

$$A_k = \sum_{i=1}^{k} \sigma_i u_i v_i^{T} = U_k \Sigma_k V_k^{T}$$

is the **best rank-$k$ approximation** to $A$ in both the **Frobenius norm** and the **spectral (2-) norm**:

$$\min_{\text{rank}(B) = k} \|A - B\|_{F} = \|A - A_k\|_{F} = \sqrt{\sigma_{k+1}^{2} + \sigma_{k+2}^{2} + \dots + \sigma_{r}^{2}}$$

**Derivation Sketch (Frobenius norm case):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\|A - A_k\|_{F}^{2} &= \left\| \sum_{i=1}^{r} \sigma_i u_i v_i^{T} - \sum_{i=1}^{k} \sigma_i u_i v_i^{T} \right\|_{F}^{2} \\
&= \left\| \sum_{i=k+1}^{r} \sigma_i u_i v_i^{T} \right\|_{F}^{2}
\end{aligned}
$$

Since $\{u_i v_i^{T}\}$ is an orthonormal set under the Frobenius inner product $\langle X, Y \rangle_F = \text{tr}(X^{T}Y)$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\left\| \sum_{i=k+1}^{r} \sigma_i u_i v_i^{T} \right\|_{F}^{2} &= \sum_{i=k+1}^{r} \sigma_i^{2} \|u_i v_i^{T}\|_{F}^{2} \\
&= \sum_{i=k+1}^{r} \sigma_i^{2} \cdot 1 = \sum_{i=k+1}^{r} \sigma_i^{2}
\end{aligned}
$$

Therefore $\|A - A_k\|_{F} = \sqrt{\sum_{i=k+1}^{r} \sigma_i^{2}}$. $\blacksquare$

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** Dropping the smallest singular values causes the **least damage**. This is the foundation of dimensionality reduction in ML — we keep the directions where $A$ "carries the most energy."

---

## 3.4 Worked Example: Best Rank-1 Approximation

**Problem:** For the $A = \begin{pmatrix} 1 & 2 \\ 2 & 1 \end{pmatrix}$ from §3.2, find the best rank-1 approximation $A_1$.

**Solution:** Keep only the largest singular value $\sigma_1 = 3$ and corresponding vectors $u_1, v_1$:

$$
A_1 = \sigma_1 u_1 v_1^{T} = 3 \cdot \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}} \begin{pmatrix} 1 \\ 1 \end{pmatrix} \cdot \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}} \begin{pmatrix} 1 & 1 \end{pmatrix} = \frac{3}{2} \begin{pmatrix} 1 & 1 \\ 1 & 1 \end{pmatrix} = \begin{pmatrix} 1.5 & 1.5 \\ 1.5 & 1.5 \end{pmatrix}
$$

**Verification of approximation error:**

$$
\|A - A_1\|_{F}^{2} = \sigma_2^{2} = 1^{2} = 1 \implies \|A - A_1\|_{F} = 1
$$

This is the **provably minimum possible** Frobenius-norm error among all rank-1 matrices.

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram: SVD Factorization Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[INPUT MATRIX A in Real MxN]
    A --> B[Compute A transpose A]
    A --> C[Compute A A transpose]

    B --> D[Eigendecomposition of A transpose A]
    C --> E[Eigendecomposition of A A transpose]

    D --> D1[Eigenvalues lambda1 ... lambdan]
    D --> D2[Eigenvectors v1 ... vn]
    D1 --> F1[Singular Values sigma i = sqrt lambda i]
    F1 --> F2[Build Diagonal Matrix Sigma in Real MxN]

    D2 --> G[Build Orthogonal Matrix V in Real NxN]

    E --> E1[Eigenvalues of A A transpose]
    E --> E2[Eigenvectors u1 ... um]
    E1 --> F1
    E2 --> H[Build Orthogonal Matrix U in Real MxM]

    F2 --> I[FINAL DECOMPOSITION A = U Sigma V transpose]
    G --> I
    H --> I

    I --> J1[Reduced SVD: keep top k singular values]
    I --> J2[Truncated SVD: rank k approximation]
    I --> J3[Moore Penrose Pseudoinverse A plus]

    style A fill:#E8F4FD,stroke:#1976D2,color:#000
    style I fill:#C8E6C9,stroke:#2E7D32,color:#000
    style F1 fill:#FFF9C4,stroke:#F9A825,color:#000
    style J1 fill:#FFE0B2,stroke:#E64A19,color:#000
    style J2 fill:#FFE0B2,stroke:#E64A19,color:#000
    style J3 fill:#FFE0B2,stroke:#E64A19,color:#000
```

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    X[Input Vector x in Real N] --> V[V transpose: Rotate Domain]
    V --> C[Coordinate transform along principal axes]
    C --> S[Sigma: Scale by sigma1 sigma2 etc]
    S --> U[U: Rotate back to Output Space]
    U --> Y[Output Vector y in Real M]

    style X fill:#E3F2FD,stroke:#0D47A1,color:#000

```

## 4.3 Singular Value Spectrum: Energy Distribution

```mermaid
graph TB
    SP[Singular Value Spectrum of A] --> SP1[sigma1: Largest - Dominant Direction]
    SP --> SP2[sigma2: Second - Important Variance]
    SP --> SP3[sigma3 .. sigmar: Minor Components]
    SP --> SP4[Beyond rank r: Zero Energy]

    SP1 --> APP1[PCA: Principal axis 1]
    SP2 --> APP2[PCA: Principal axis 2]
    SP3 --> APP3[PCA: Higher order axes - often truncated]
    SP4 --> APP4[Null space - discard]

    SP1 --> IMG[Image Compression: keep]
    SP3 --> IMG2[Image Compression: discard below threshold]
```

## 4.4 Connection Map: SVD ↔ Other Factorizations

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    SVD[Singular Value Decomposition A = U Sigma V transpose]

    SVD --> EIG1[Special case: Symmetric A gives SVD = Eigendecomposition]
    SVD --> EIG2[A transpose A = V Sigma transpose Sigma V transpose - symmetric eigendecomp]
    SVD --> EIG3[A A transpose = U Sigma Sigma transpose U transpose - symmetric eigendecomp]

    SVD --> POLAR[Polar Decomposition A = Q H where Q is orthogonal and H is PSD]
```

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A.1 [KTU University Exam – May 2024]

**Q: Define Singular Value Decomposition (SVD) of a rectangular matrix. State the dimensions of the three component matrices when $A \in \mathbb{R}^{m \times n}$.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> [!NOTE]
> **SVD Definition:** Any real matrix $A \in \mathbb{R}^{m \times n}$ can be factored as
> $$A = U \Sigma V^{T}$$
> where $U \in \mathbb{R}^{m \times m}$ and $V \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times n}$ are **orthogonal matrices**, and $\Sigma \in \mathbb{R}^{m \times n}$ is a **rectangular diagonal matrix** whose non-negative diagonal entries $\sigma_1 \geq \sigma_2 \geq \dots \geq \sigma_r > 0$ are called the **singular values** of $A$ (where $r = \text{rank}(A)$).
>
> **Dimensions:** $U$ is $m \times m$, $\Sigma$ is $m \times n$, $V$ is $n \times n$ (so $V^{T}$ is $n \times n$). The product $U \Sigma V^{T}$ has shape $m \times n$, matching $A$. (1 mark for definition, 1 mark for naming components, 1 mark for dimensions)

---

### Question A.2 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]

**Q: State any four important properties of the singular values of a matrix $A$.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> 1. **Non-negativity:** All singular values are real and satisfy $\sigma_i \geq 0$. (1 mark)
> 2. **Count = Rank:** The number of non-zero singular values equals the rank of $A$: $\text{rank}(A) = \#\{i : \sigma_i > 0\}$. (0.5 mark)
> 3. **Eigenvalue link:** $\sigma_i^2$ are the eigenvalues of $A^{T}A$ (or $AA^{T}$). (0.5 mark)
> 4. **Ordering:** By convention, $\sigma_1 \geq \sigma_2 \geq \dots \geq \sigma_r > 0$. (0.5 mark)
> 5. **Norm equalities:** $\|A\|_{F} = \sqrt{\sum \sigma_i^2}$ and $\|A\|_2 = \sigma_1$. (0.5 mark, for any one of these or any other valid property)

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Do NOT confuse eigenvalues with singular values.** Eigenvalues can be complex; singular values are real and non-negative. (Common: 1–2 mark deduction)
> 2. **Always verify orthogonality** of $U$ and $V$ at the end. Skipping this loses 2 marks on board exams.
> 3. **Dimension mismatches** in $\Sigma$ are the #1 reason for zero scores. For an $m \times n$ matrix, $\Sigma$ must also be $m \times n$, with $\sigma_i$ on the main diagonal and zeros elsewhere.
> 4. **Sorting order matters:** $\sigma_1 \geq \sigma_2$. Reversing the order means $U$, $V$ columns must also be reordered to match.
> 5. **Do not forget the transpose** in $A = U\Sigma V^{T}$. Writing $A = U\Sigma V$ (without transpose) is **wrong** and will cost 1 mark.

---

### Question B — Option (A) [14 Marks] [KTU University Exam – Sample Paper 2024]

**(a)** Find the Singular Value Decomposition of the matrix:
$$A = \begin{pmatrix} 1 & 2 \\ 2 & 1 \end{pmatrix}$$
**(7 marks)**

**(b)** Using the SVD obtained in part (a), find the **best rank-1 approximation** of $A$ in the Frobenius norm, and compute the approximation error. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 marks]

**Step 1: Compute $A^{T}A$** *(1 mark)*

$$
A^{T}A = \begin{pmatrix} 1 & 2 \\ 2 & 1 \end{pmatrix} \begin{pmatrix} 1 & 2 \\ 2 & 1 \end{pmatrix} = \begin{pmatrix} 5 & 4 \\ 4 & 5 \end{pmatrix}
$$

**Step 2: Eigenvalues of $A^{T}A$** *(1.5 marks)*

$$
\det \begin{pmatrix} 5 - \lambda & 4 \\ 4 & 5 - \lambda \end{pmatrix} = (5 - \lambda)^2 - 16 = 0
$$
$$
\lambda_1 = 9, \quad \lambda_2 = 1
$$

**Step 3: Singular values and $\Sigma$** *(0.5 mark)*

$$
\sigma_1 = 3, \quad \sigma_2 = 1, \quad \Sigma = \begin{pmatrix} 3 & 0 \\ 0 & 1 \end{pmatrix}
$$

**Step 4: Right singular vectors (eigenvectors of $A^{T}A$)** *(1.5 marks)*

For $\lambda_1 = 9$: $v_1 = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}} \begin{pmatrix} 1 \\ 1 \end{pmatrix}$

For $\lambda_2 = 1$: $v_2 = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}} \begin{pmatrix} 1 \\ -1 \end{pmatrix}$

$$
V = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}} \begin{pmatrix} 1 & 1 \\ 1 & -1 \end{pmatrix}
$$

**Step 5: Left singular vectors** *(1.5 marks)*

$$
u_1 = \frac{1}{\sigma_1} A v_1 = \frac{1}{3} \cdot \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}} \begin{pmatrix} 3 \\ 3 \end{pmatrix} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}} \begin{pmatrix} 1 \\ 1 \end{pmatrix}
$$

$$
u_2 = \frac{1}{\sigma_2} A v_2 = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}} \begin{pmatrix} -1 \\ 1 \end{pmatrix}
$$

$$
U = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}} \begin{pmatrix} 1 & -1 \\ 1 & 1 \end{pmatrix}
$$

**Step 6: Verification** *(1 mark)*

$$
U \Sigma V^{T} = \frac{1}{2} \begin{pmatrix} 3 & -1 \\ 3 & 1 \end{pmatrix} \begin{pmatrix} 1 & 1 \\ 1 & -1 \end{pmatrix} = \begin{pmatrix} 1 & 2 \\ 2 & 1 \end{pmatrix} = A \quad \checkmark
$$

**Final Answer:**
$$
\boxed{A = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}} \begin{pmatrix} 1 & -1 \\ 1 & 1 \end{pmatrix} \begin{pmatrix} 3 & 0 \\ 0 & 1 \end{pmatrix} \cdot \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}} \begin{pmatrix} 1 & 1 \\ 1 & -1 \end{pmatrix}}
$$

---

#### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 marks]

**Step 1: Best rank-1 formula** *(1 mark)*

The Eckart–Young theorem states that the best rank-$k$ approximation (in Frobenius norm) is:
$$A_k = \sum_{i=1}^{k} \sigma_i u_i v_i^{T}$$

For $k = 1$:
$$A_1 = \sigma_1 u_1 v_1^{T}$$

**Step 2: Compute $A_1$** *(2 marks)*

$$
A_1 = 3 \cdot \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}} \begin{pmatrix} 1 \\ 1 \end{pmatrix} \cdot \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}} \begin{pmatrix} 1 & 1 \end{pmatrix} = \frac{3}{2} \begin{pmatrix} 1 & 1 \\ 1 & 1 \end{pmatrix} = \begin{pmatrix} 1.5 & 1.5 \\ 1.5 & 1.5 \end{pmatrix}
$$

**Step 3: Compute the residual** *(2 marks)*

$$
A - A_1 = \begin{pmatrix} 1 - 1.5 & 2 - 1.5 \\ 2 - 1.5 & 1 - 1.5 \end{pmatrix} = \begin{pmatrix} -0.5 & 0.5 \\ 0.5 & -0.5 \end{pmatrix}
$$

**Step 4: Frobenius error** *(1 mark)*

$$
\|A - A_1\|_{F} = \sqrt{(-0.5)^2 + (0.5)^2 + (0.5)^2 + (-0.5)^2} = \sqrt{1.0} = 1
$$

**Step 5: Confirm via theorem** *(1 mark)*

By Eckart–Young, the minimum error is $\sqrt{\sum_{i=2}^{r} \sigma_i^2} = \sigma_2 = 1$. ✓

---

### Question B — Option (B) [14 Marks] [KTU University Exam – Sample Paper 2024]

**(a)** Find the Singular Value Decomposition of the matrix:
$$A = \begin{pmatrix} 3 & 0 \\ 4 & 5 \end{pmatrix}$$
**(7 marks)**

**(b)** Prove that the non-zero singular values of $A$ are the same as the non-zero eigenvalues of $A^{T}A$ (or $AA^{T}$). Hence show that $\|A\|_{F}^{2} = \sum_{i} \sigma_i^{2}$. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 marks]

**Step 1: Compute $A^{T}A$** *(1 mark)*

$$
A^{T}A = \begin{pmatrix} 3 & 4 \\ 0 & 5 \end{pmatrix} \begin{pmatrix} 3 & 0 \\ 4 & 5 \end{pmatrix} = \begin{pmatrix} 25 & 20 \\ 20 & 25 \end{pmatrix}
$$

**Step 2: Eigenvalues** *(1.5 marks)*

$$
\det \begin{pmatrix} 25 - \lambda & 20 \\ 20 & 25 - \lambda \end{pmatrix} = (25 - \lambda)^2 - 400 = 0
$$
$$
(25 - \lambda)^2 = 400 \implies 25 - \lambda = \pm 20
$$
$$
\lambda_1 = 45, \quad \lambda_2 = 5
$$

**Step 3: Singular values and $\Sigma$** *(0.5 mark)*

$$
\sigma_1 = \sqrt{45} = 3\sqrt{5}, \quad \sigma_2 = \sqrt{5}, \quad \Sigma = \begin{pmatrix} 3\sqrt{5} & 0 \\ 0 & \sqrt{5} \end{pmatrix}
$$

**Step 4: Right singular vectors** *(1.5 marks)*

For $\lambda_1 = 45$: $\begin{pmatrix} -20 & 20 \\ 20 & -20 \end{pmatrix} v_1 = 0 \Rightarrow v_1 = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}} \begin{pmatrix} 1 \\ 1 \end{pmatrix}$

For $\lambda_2 = 5$: $\begin{pmatrix} 20 & 20 \\ 20 & 20 \end{pmatrix} v_2 = 0 \Rightarrow v_2 = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}} \begin{pmatrix} 1 \\ -1 \end{pmatrix}$

$$
V = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}} \begin{pmatrix} 1 & 1 \\ 1 & -1 \end{pmatrix}
$$

**Step 5: Left singular vectors** *(1.5 marks)*

$$
u_1 = \frac{1}{3\sqrt{5}} A v_1 = \frac{1}{3\sqrt{5}} \cdot \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}} \begin{pmatrix} 3 \\ 9 \end{pmatrix} = \frac{1}{3\sqrt{10}} \begin{pmatrix} 3 \\ 9 \end{pmatrix} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{10}} \begin{pmatrix} 1 \\ 3 \end{pmatrix}
$$

$$
u_2 = \frac{1}{\sqrt{5}} A v_2 = \frac{1}{\sqrt{5}} \cdot \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}} \begin{pmatrix} 3 \\ -1 \end{pmatrix} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{10}} \begin{pmatrix} 3 \\ -1 \end{pmatrix}
$$

$$
U = \frac{1}{\sqrt{10}} \begin{pmatrix} 1 & 3 \\ 3 & -1 \end{pmatrix}
$$

**Step 6: Final SVD and verification** *(1 mark)*

$$
\boxed{A = \frac{1}{\sqrt{10}} \begin{pmatrix} 1 & 3 \\ 3 & -1 \end{pmatrix} \begin{pmatrix} 3\sqrt{5} & 0 \\ 0 & \sqrt{5} \end{pmatrix} \cdot \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}} \begin{pmatrix} 1 & 1 \\ 1 & -1 \end{pmatrix}}
$$

(Verification: $U$ and $V$ are orthogonal, $\Sigma$ has correct dimensions.)

---

#### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 marks]

**Theorem:** The non-zero singular values of $A$ are the positive square roots of the non-zero eigenvalues of $A^{T}A$.

**Proof:** *(3.5 marks)*

Assume $A \in \mathbb{R}^{m \times n}$ has SVD $A = U \Sigma V^{T}$. Then:

$$
\begin{aligned}
A^{T}A &= (U \Sigma V^{T})^{T} (U \Sigma V^{T}) \\
&= V \Sigma^{T} U^{T} U \Sigma V^{T} \\
&= V \Sigma^{T} \Sigma V^{T} && \text{(since } U^{T}U = I_m)
\end{aligned}
$$

This is the eigendecomposition of $A^{T}A$, with $V$ orthogonal and $\Sigma^{T}\Sigma = \text{diag}(\sigma_1^2, \sigma_2^2, \dots, \sigma_n^2)$. The eigenvalues of $A^{T}A$ are exactly $\sigma_i^2$ (and 0 for the indices $i > r$ where $\sigma_i = 0$).

**Conversely:** If $A^{T}A v = \lambda v$ with $\lambda > 0$ and $\|v\| = 1$:

$$
\|Av\|^{2} = v^{T} A^{T} A v = v^{T} (\lambda v) = \lambda
$$

So $\|Av\| = \sqrt{\lambda} > 0$. Setting $u = \frac{Av}{\|Av\|}$ gives $Av = \sqrt{\lambda} u$, and $\sqrt{\lambda}$ is a singular value of $A$. $\blacksquare$

**Frobenius norm result:** *(2 marks)*

$$
\begin{aligned}
\|A\|_{F}^{2} &= \text{tr}(A^{T}A) \\
&= \text{tr}(V \Sigma^{T} \Sigma V^{T}) \\
&= \text{tr}(\Sigma^{T} \Sigma V^{T} V) && \text{(cyclic property of trace)} \\
&= \text{tr}(\Sigma^{T} \Sigma) \\
&= \sum_{i=1}^{r} \sigma_i^2
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conclusion:** *(1.5 marks)*

The same argument applied to $AA^{T}$ shows its eigenvalues are also $\{\sigma_1^2, \sigma_2^2, \dots, \sigma_r^2, 0, \dots, 0\}$. Hence $A^{T}A$ and $AA^{T}$ have identical non-zero eigenvalues — this is the foundation of the link between left and right singular vectors.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT] > **Last-Minute Revision Checklist for KTU 2024 SVD Questions**

- **Definition:** Any $A \in \mathbb{R}^{m \times n}$ decomposes as $A = U\Sigma V^{T}$ with $U, V$ orthogonal, $\Sigma$ rectangular diagonal of $\sigma_i \geq 0$. (1 mark question guaranteed.)
- **Singular values = $\sqrt{\lambda_i(A^{T}A)}$:** Always compute $A^{T}A$ **first** when asked to find SVD. *(Most common 14-mark workflow: $A \to A^{T}A \to \lambda_i \to v_i \to u_i$.)*
- **Ordering:** $\sigma_1 \geq \sigma_2 \geq \dots \geq \sigma_r > 0$. Reorder consistently across $U$, $\Sigma$, $V$.
- **Left singular vectors:** Use $u_i = \frac{1}{\sigma_i} A v_i$ for $i = 1, \dots, r$ (only when $\sigma_i \neq 0$).
- **Rank:** $\text{rank}(A) = \#\{i : \sigma_i > 0\}$.
- **Norms:** $\|A\|_{F}^{2} = \sum \sigma_i^2$ and $\|A\|_2 = \sigma_1$.
- **Determinant (square case):** $\det(A) = \prod_{i=1}^{n} \sigma_i$ (with sign: $\pm$ depending on $\det(UV^{T})$).
- **Pseudoinverse:** $A^{+} = V \Sigma^{+} U^{T}$, where $\Sigma^{+}$ has $1/\sigma_i$ for non-zero entries (and 0 otherwise).
- **Eckart–Young:** Best rank-$k$ approximation is $A_k = U_k \Sigma_k V_k^{T}$, with error $\sqrt{\sum_{i>k} \sigma_i^2}$.
- **ML Connection:** Right singular vectors $V$ are the **principal components** in PCA. Variance captured by $i$-th component = $\sigma_i^2 / \sum_j \sigma_j^2$.
- **Polar Decomposition:** $A = QH$ where $Q = UV^{T}$ (orthogonal) and $H = V \Sigma V^{T}$ (symmetric PSD).
- **Computational Tool:** In practice, SVD is computed via numerical algorithms (Golub–Kahan bidiagonalization + QR iteration), **never** by hand for large matrices.

> [!NOTE] > **Closing Tip:** When solving KTU SVD problems, always draw a small "checklist" on the side: (1) $A^{T}A$ computed? (2) Eigenvalues found? (3) Eigenvectors normalized? (4) $U, V$ orthogonal? (5) $U \Sigma V^{T} = A$ verified? Each tick = marks secured. Good luck! 🎯

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Orthogonal Projection
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAET753 - LINEAR ALGEBRA IN AI AND ML
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** Orthogonal Projection

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition

An **Orthogonal Projection** is a linear transformation $P : \mathbb{R}^n \to \mathbb{R}^n$ that maps any vector onto a subspace $W$ in such a way that the line connecting the original vector to its image is **perpendicular (orthogonal)** to $W$. Formally, for a vector $\mathbf{x} \in \mathbb{R}^n$ and a subspace $W \subseteq \mathbb{R}^n$, the orthogonal projection of $\mathbf{x}$ onto $W$, denoted $\text{proj}_W(\mathbf{x})$, is the unique vector $\hat{\mathbf{x}} \in W$ such that $\mathbf{x} - \hat{\mathbf{x}} \perp W$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Module 3 Core):**
> An orthogonal projection onto a subspace $W$ of $\mathbb{R}^n$ is a linear operator $P \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times n}$ satisfying two conditions simultaneously:
> 1. **Idempotency:** $P^2 = P$
> 2. **Symmetry:** $P^T = P$
>
> Any matrix satisfying both conditions is a *projection matrix*, and when $P^T = P$ is also satisfied, the projection is *orthogonal*.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — The "Sunlight and Shadow" Model** ☀️🪑
>
> Imagine a sunny day at noon, with the sun directly overhead casting **vertical** shadows of every object onto the ground. Now consider a chair in a room:
> - The **floor** is our subspace $W$ (a 2D plane in 3D space).
> - The **top of the chair** is our vector $\mathbf{x}$ (a point in 3D).
> - The **shadow** of the chair-top on the floor is $\text{proj}_W(\mathbf{x})$.
> - The light ray connecting the chair-top to its shadow is $\mathbf{x} - \hat{\mathbf{x}}$, and it is **perfectly perpendicular** to the floor.
>
> The "shadow" is the *closest* point on the floor to the chair-top. This is the geometric essence of orthogonal projection: **finding the closest point in a subspace**.

### 1.3 Why Orthogonal Projection Matters in AI/ML

| AI/ML Application | Role of Orthogonal Projection |
|---|---|
| **Principal Component Analysis (PCA)** | Projects data onto principal axes (eigenvectors) to reduce dimensionality |
| **Linear Regression (Least Squares)** | Projects $y$ onto column space of design matrix $A$ |
| **Neural Network Initialization** | Orthogonal weight matrices preserve gradient norm |
| **Recommender Systems** | Low-rank matrix approximations via projection |
| **Word Embeddings** | Removing unwanted components (e.g., gender bias removal) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Numerical Metric:** The error vector $\mathbf{e} = \mathbf{x} - \hat{\mathbf{x}}$ is always orthogonal to $W$, meaning $\langle \mathbf{e}, \mathbf{w} \rangle = 0$ for every $\mathbf{w} \in W$.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The 'Why' and 'How' Behind Orthogonal Projection

The orthogonal projection operates on a precise sequence of logical steps:

- **Step 1 — Identify the Subspace $W$:** Determine a subspace $W$ (often a line, plane, or hyperplane) onto which we want to project.
- **Step 2 — Choose an Orthogonal Basis for $W$:** Construct or identify a set of orthonormal vectors $\{\mathbf{u}_1, \mathbf{u}_2, \dots, \mathbf{u}_k\}$ that span $W$.
- **Step 3 — Compute Component-wise Projections:** For each basis vector $\mathbf{u}_i$, compute the scalar component $(\mathbf{u}_i^T \mathbf{x})$ that measures the "shadow length" along $\mathbf{u}_i$.
- **Step 4 — Reconstruct the Projection Vector:** Sum the weighted basis vectors: $\hat{\mathbf{x}} = \sum_{i=1}^{k} (\mathbf{u}_i^T \mathbf{x}) \, \mathbf{u}_i$.
- **Step 5 — Verify Orthogonality:** Confirm that $\mathbf{x} - \hat{\mathbf{x}}$ is perpendicular to every vector in $W$, i.e., $\mathbf{u}_i^T(\mathbf{x} - \hat{\mathbf{x}}) = 0$ for all $i$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why does this work?** The Pythagorean theorem in vector form guarantees that the distance $\Vert \mathbf{x} - \hat{\mathbf{x}} \Vert$ is **minimized** when the connecting vector is perpendicular to $W$. This is the foundation of the **Least Squares Method**.

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Property | Mathematical Expression | Condition / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Projection onto a line (non-zero vector $\mathbf{v}$) | $\text{proj}_{\mathbf{v}} \mathbf{x} = \dfrac{\mathbf{v} \mathbf{v}^T}{\mathbf{v}^T \mathbf{v}} \mathbf{x}$ | When $\mathbf{v}$ is not normalized |
| 2 | Projection onto a unit vector $\mathbf{u}$ | $\text{proj}_{\mathbf{u}} \mathbf{x} = (\mathbf{u}^T \mathbf{x}) \mathbf{u}$ | When $\Vert \mathbf{u} \Vert = 1$ |
| 3 | Projection matrix (line) | $P = \dfrac{\mathbf{v} \mathbf{v}^T}{\mathbf{v}^T \mathbf{v}}$ | Symmetric, idempotent |
| 4 | Projection matrix (subspace, orthonormal basis $Q$) | $P = Q Q^T$ | $Q \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times k}$ with $Q^T Q = I_k$ |
| 5 | Projection matrix (general basis $A$) | $P = A (A^T A)^{-1} A^T$ | $A \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times k}$ has full column rank |
| 6 | Idempotency | $P^2 = P$ | Defining property of a projection |
| 7 | Symmetry (orthogonal case) | $P^T = P$ | Distinguishes orthogonal from oblique |
| 8 | Orthogonality of error | $(\mathbf{x} - P\mathbf{x}) \perp W$ | Consequence of $P^T = P$ |
| 9 | Distance from $\mathbf{x}$ to $W$ | $\Vert \mathbf{x} - P\mathbf{x} \Vert = \sqrt{\mathbf{x}^T \mathbf{x} - \mathbf{x}^T P \mathbf{x}}$ | Standard metric in $\mathbb{R}^n$ |
| 10 | Complementarity with $I - P$ | $(I - P)\mathbf{x}$ is the **rejection** vector | Always perpendicular to $\hat{\mathbf{x}}$ |
| 11 | Eigenvalues of $P$ | $\lambda \in \{0, 1\}$ | Exactly $\text{rank}(P)$ eigenvalues equal 1, rest equal 0 |
| 12 | Trace identity | $\text{tr}(P) = \text{rank}(P) = \dim(W)$ | Useful in PCA reconstruction error |

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Signal Processing:** Removing noise from a signal by projecting onto the signal subspace (e.g., **MUSIC algorithm** for direction-of-arrival estimation in radar).
- **Computer Graphics:** Flattening 3D objects onto 2D screens via **orthographic projection** (used in CAD, engineering drawings).
- **Computer Vision:** Camera calibration relies on projecting 3D world points onto a 2D image plane.
- **Quantum Mechanics:** Measurement operators are **orthogonal projection operators** in Hilbert space.
- **Statistics:** The **hat matrix** $H = X(X^TX)^{-1}X^T$ in linear regression is an orthogonal projection onto the column space of $X$.

> [!TIP]
> **Exam Tip:** If a problem gives you a *non-orthonormal* basis $A$, always convert to orthonormal form $Q$ using Gram-Schmidt before applying $P = QQ^T$. Using $P = A(A^TA)^{-1}A^T$ is computationally equivalent but more prone to numerical errors in higher dimensions.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Projection onto a Line Spanned by a Non-Zero Vector

**Problem Setup:** Given a non-zero vector $\mathbf{v} \in \mathbb{R}^n$ and an arbitrary vector $\mathbf{x} \in \mathbb{R}^n$, find $\hat{\mathbf{x}} = \text{proj}_{\mathbf{v}} \mathbf{x}$.

**Logical Foundation:** The projection $\hat{\mathbf{x}}$ must lie on the line spanned by $\mathbf{v}$, so $\hat{\mathbf{x}} = c \mathbf{v}$ for some scalar $c \in \mathbb{R}$. Additionally, the error $\mathbf{x} - \hat{\mathbf{x}}$ must be orthogonal to $\mathbf{v}$.

**Step-by-Step Derivation:**

Starting from the orthogonality condition:

$$
\mathbf{v}^T (\mathbf{x} - \hat{\mathbf{x}}) = 0
$$

Substituting $\hat{\mathbf{x}} = c \mathbf{v}$:

$$
\mathbf{v}^T (\mathbf{x} - c \mathbf{v}) = 0
$$

Expanding the dot product:

$$
\mathbf{v}^T \mathbf{x} - c \, \mathbf{v}^T \mathbf{v} = 0
$$

Solving for the scalar $c$:

$$
c = \frac{\mathbf{v}^T \mathbf{x}}{\mathbf{v}^T \mathbf{v}}
$$

Substituting $c$ back into $\hat{\mathbf{x}} = c \mathbf{v}$:

$$
\hat{\mathbf{x}} = \frac{\mathbf{v}^T \mathbf{x}}{\mathbf{v}^T \mathbf{v}} \, \mathbf{v}
$$

Rewriting in matrix form by absorbing the scalar into a matrix:

$$
\hat{\mathbf{x}} = \left( \frac{\mathbf{v} \mathbf{v}^T}{\mathbf{v}^T \mathbf{v}} \right) \mathbf{x}
$$

**Conclusion:**

$$
\boxed{P = \frac{\mathbf{v} \mathbf{v}^T}{\mathbf{v}^T \mathbf{v}}}
$$

is the projection matrix onto $\text{span}(\mathbf{v})$, and it satisfies both $P^2 = P$ and $P^T = P$.

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Projection onto a Subspace (General Basis $A$)

**Problem Setup:** Let $W = \text{col}(A)$ where $A \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times k}$ has rank $k$ (full column rank). Find $P$ such that $\hat{\mathbf{x}} = P \mathbf{x} \in W$ and $\mathbf{x} - P\mathbf{x} \perp W$.

**Step-by-Step Derivation:**

Since $\hat{\mathbf{x}} \in W = \text{col}(A)$, we can write $\hat{\mathbf{x}} = A \mathbf{y}$ for some $\mathbf{y} \in \mathbb{R}^k$.

Orthogonality condition: $(\mathbf{x} - A\mathbf{y}) \perp \text{col}(A)$ implies $A^T(\mathbf{x} - A\mathbf{y}) = \mathbf{0}$.

Expanding:

$$
A^T \mathbf{x} - A^T A \mathbf{y} = \mathbf{0}
$$

Since $A$ has full column rank, $A^T A$ is invertible:

$$
A^T A \mathbf{y} = A^T \mathbf{x}
$$

Solving the **normal equations**:

$$
\mathbf{y} = (A^T A)^{-1} A^T \mathbf{x}
$$

Substituting back into $\hat{\mathbf{x}} = A \mathbf{y}$:

$$
\hat{\mathbf{x}} = A (A^T A)^{-1} A^T \mathbf{x}
$$

**Conclusion:**

$$
\boxed{P = A (A^T A)^{-1} A^T}
$$

is the orthogonal projection matrix onto the column space of $A$.

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Projection onto a Subspace (Orthonormal Basis $Q$)

**Problem Setup:** Let $Q \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times k}$ have orthonormal columns, so $Q^T Q = I_k$. Project $\mathbf{x}$ onto $W = \text{col}(Q)$.

**Step-by-Step Derivation:**

Using the general formula with $A = Q$:

$$
P = Q (Q^T Q)^{-1} Q^T
$$

Since $Q^T Q = I_k$, its inverse is also $I_k$:

$$
(Q^T Q)^{-1} = I_k^{-1} = I_k
$$

Substituting:

$$
P = Q \cdot I_k \cdot Q^T = Q Q^T
$$

**Conclusion:**

$$
\boxed{P = Q Q^T}
$$

is the simplified form of the orthogonal projection matrix when using an orthonormal basis $Q$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Proof of Idempotency ($P^2 = P$) for $P = QQ^T$:**
>
> $$
> P^2 = (QQ^T)(QQ^T) = Q (Q^T Q) Q^T = Q \cdot I_k \cdot Q^T = QQ^T = P
> $$
>
> **Proof of Symmetry ($P^T = P$) for $P = QQ^T$:**
>
> $$
> P^T = (QQ^T)^T = (Q^T)^T Q^T = QQ^T = P
> $$

### 3.4 Derivation 4 — Minimum Distance Property

**Theorem:** Among all vectors $\mathbf{w} \in W$, the orthogonal projection $\hat{\mathbf{x}} = P\mathbf{x}$ minimizes the distance $\Vert \mathbf{x} - \mathbf{w} \Vert$.

**Proof:** Let $\mathbf{w} \in W$ be any vector in the subspace. Since both $\hat{\mathbf{x}}$ and $\mathbf{w}$ lie in $W$, their difference $\hat{\mathbf{x}} - \mathbf{w} \in W$. By the orthogonality of the error to $W$:

$$
(\mathbf{x} - \hat{\mathbf{x}}) \perp (\hat{\mathbf{x}} - \mathbf{w})
$$

Applying the Pythagorean theorem:

$$
\Vert \mathbf{x} - \mathbf{w} \Vert^2 = \Vert (\mathbf{x} - \hat{\mathbf{x}}) + (\hat{\mathbf{x}} - \mathbf{w}) \Vert^2
$$

Expanding:

$$
\Vert \mathbf{x} - \mathbf{w} \Vert^2 = \Vert \mathbf{x} - \hat{\mathbf{x}} \Vert^2 + 2(\mathbf{x} - \hat{\mathbf{x}})^T(\hat{\mathbf{x}} - \mathbf{w}) + \Vert \hat{\mathbf{x}} - \mathbf{w} \Vert^2
$$

The middle term is zero by orthogonality:

$$
\Vert \mathbf{x} - \mathbf{w} \Vert^2 = \Vert \mathbf{x} - \hat{\mathbf{x}} \Vert^2 + \Vert \hat{\mathbf{x}} - \mathbf{w} \Vert^2
$$

Since $\Vert \hat{\mathbf{x}} - \mathbf{w} \Vert^2 \geq 0$:

$$
\Vert \mathbf{x} - \mathbf{w} \Vert^2 \geq \Vert \mathbf{x} - \hat{\mathbf{x}} \Vert^2
$$

$$
\boxed{\Vert \mathbf{x} - \mathbf{w} \Vert \geq \Vert \mathbf{x} - \hat{\mathbf{x}} \Vert \quad \text{for all } \mathbf{w} \in W}
$$

This proves that $\hat{\mathbf{x}}$ is the unique closest point in $W$ to $\mathbf{x}$.

### 3.5 Analytical Worked Example

**Problem:** Find the orthogonal projection of $\mathbf{x} = (3, 4, 5)^T$ onto the plane $W$ spanned by $\mathbf{v}_1 = (1, 0, 1)^T$ and $\mathbf{v}_2 = (1, 1, 0)^T$.

**Step 1 — Build the matrix $A$:**

$$
A = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 1 \\ 0 & 1 \\ 1 & 0 \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute $A^T A$:**

$$
A^T A = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 & 1 \\ 1 & 1 & 0 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 1 \\ 0 & 1 \\ 1 & 0 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 2 & 1 \\ 1 & 2 \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute $(A^T A)^{-1}$:**

$$
\det(A^T A) = (2)(2) - (1)(1) = 4 - 1 = 3
$$

$$
(A^T A)^{-1} = \frac{1}{3} \begin{bmatrix} 2 & -1 \\ -1 & 2 \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step 4 — Compute $A^T \mathbf{x}$:**

$$
A^T \mathbf{x} = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 & 1 \\ 1 & 1 & 0 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} 3 \\ 4 \\ 5 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 8 \\ 7 \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step 5 — Compute $\mathbf{y} = (A^TA)^{-1} A^T \mathbf{x}$:**

$$
\mathbf{y} = \frac{1}{3} \begin{bmatrix} 2 & -1 \\ -1 & 2 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} 8 \\ 7 \end{bmatrix} = \frac{1}{3} \begin{bmatrix} 16 - 7 \\ -8 + 14 \end{bmatrix} = \frac{1}{3} \begin{bmatrix} 9 \\ 6 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 3 \\ 2 \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step 6 — Compute $\hat{\mathbf{x}} = A\mathbf{y}$:**

$$
\hat{\mathbf{x}} = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 1 \\ 0 & 1 \\ 1 & 0 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} 3 \\ 2 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 5 \\ 2 \\ 3 \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step 7 — Verify Orthogonality:**

$$
\mathbf{e} = \mathbf{x} - \hat{\mathbf{x}} = \begin{bmatrix} 3 \\ 4 \\ 5 \end{bmatrix} - \begin{bmatrix} 5 \\ 2 \\ 3 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} -2 \\ 2 \\ 2 \end{bmatrix}
$$

Check $\mathbf{v}_1^T \mathbf{e} = (1)(−2) + (0)(2) + (1)(2) = 0$ ✓
Check $\mathbf{v}_2^T \mathbf{e} = (1)(−2) + (1)(2) + (0)(2) = 0$ ✓

**Final Answer:**

$$
\boxed{\hat{\mathbf{x}} = (5, 2, 3)^T, \quad \text{Error} = (-2, 2, 2)^T, \quad \Vert \text{Error} \Vert = 2\sqrt{3}}
$$

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Diagram — Orthogonal Projection Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["INPUT VECTOR x in R^n"] --> B{"Identify Subspace W"}
    B --> C["Line Case: dim W = 1"]
    B --> D["Plane Case: dim W = 2"]
    B --> E["Hyperplane Case: dim W = n minus 1"]
    C --> F["Formula: P = v v transpose over v transpose v"]
    D --> G["Formula: P = Q Q transpose with orthonormal Q"]
    E --> H["Formula: P = I minus n n transpose over n transpose n"]
    F --> I["Compute proj W of x = P times x"]
    G --> I
    H --> I
    I --> J{"Verify Two Properties"}
    J --> K["Check Idempotency P squared equals P"]
    J --> L["Check Symmetry P transpose equals P"]
    K --> M["OUTPUT PROJECTED VECTOR x hat in W"]
    L --> M
    M --> N["Error Vector e = x minus x hat"]
    N --> O["Final Verification: e perpendicular to W"]
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Diagram — Geometric Interpretation Block

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Space3D["Three Dimensional Space R cubed"]
        V1["Original Vector x at coordinates a, b, c"]
        V2["Projected Vector x hat on plane W"]
        V3["Error Vector e perpendicular to W"]
        V1 -->|"orthogonal drop"| V2
        V1 -->|"residual component"| V3
        V2 -->|"right angle at foot"| V3
    end
    V2 --> OUT["OUTPUT: Closest point in W to x"]
    V3 --> ERR["OUTPUT: Minimum distance error"]
```

### 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture (Projection in ML Pipeline)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    DATA["High Dimensional Input Data n dimensions"] --> COV["Compute Covariance Matrix C"]
    COV --> EIG["Eigendecomposition C v equals lambda v"]
    EIG --> SEL["Select Top k Eigenvectors Q matrix"]
    SEL --> PROJ["Apply Projection P equals Q Q transpose"]
    PROJ --> LOW["Low Dimensional Representation k dimensions"]
    LOW --> RECON["Reconstruct x hat equals P x"]
    RECON --> LOSS["Compute Reconstruction Loss x minus x hat squared"]
    LOSS --> OUT["OUTPUT: Compressed Feature Vector"]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Interpretation:** The third diagram is a direct visual representation of **Principal Component Analysis (PCA)** — the most important AI/ML application of orthogonal projection. Each block is a fundamental operation: covariance reveals directions of maximum variance, eigendecomposition finds principal axes, and orthogonal projection reduces dimensions while preserving maximum information.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`** Define an orthogonal projection matrix. State the two conditions that a matrix $P$ must satisfy to be an orthogonal projection matrix. Verify whether the matrix $P = \begin{bmatrix} 1/2 & 1/2 \\ 1/2 & 1/2 \end{bmatrix}$ is an orthogonal projection matrix.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

An **orthogonal projection matrix** is a square matrix $P$ that projects vectors onto a subspace $W$ such that the error component is orthogonal to $W$.

**Two conditions:**
1. **Idempotency:** $P^2 = P$
2. **Symmetry:** $P^T = P$

**Verification for given $P$:**

**Check Idempotency:**

$$
P^2 = \begin{bmatrix} 1/2 & 1/2 \\ 1/2 & 1/2 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} 1/2 & 1/2 \\ 1/2 & 1/2 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 1/4+1/4 & 1/4+1/4 \\ 1/4+1/4 & 1/4+1/4 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 1/2 & 1/2 \\ 1/2 & 1/2 \end{bmatrix} = P \quad \checkmark
$$

**Check Symmetry:** $P$ is clearly symmetric since $P_{ij} = P_{ji}$ for all $i, j$. ✓

Since both conditions are satisfied, **$P$ is an orthogonal projection matrix**.

---

**Q2. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`** Given $\mathbf{v} = (1, 2, 2)^T$ and $\mathbf{x} = (2, 1, 3)^T$, compute the orthogonal projection of $\mathbf{x}$ onto the line spanned by $\mathbf{v}$.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Step 1:** Compute $\mathbf{v}^T \mathbf{v}$:

$$
\mathbf{v}^T \mathbf{v} = 1^2 + 2^2 + 2^2 = 1 + 4 + 4 = 9
$$

**Step 2:** Compute $\mathbf{v}^T \mathbf{x}$:

$$
\mathbf{v}^T \mathbf{x} = (1)(2) + (2)(1) + (2)(3) = 2 + 2 + 6 = 10
$$

**Step 3:** Apply the projection formula:

$$
\hat{\mathbf{x}} = \frac{\mathbf{v}^T \mathbf{x}}{\mathbf{v}^T \mathbf{v}} \mathbf{v} = \frac{10}{9} \begin{bmatrix} 1 \\ 2 \\ 2 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 10/9 \\ 20/9 \\ 20/9 \end{bmatrix}
$$

$$
\boxed{\hat{\mathbf{x}} = \left( \frac{10}{9}, \frac{20}{9}, \frac{20}{9} \right)^T}
$$

---

### Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

---

## **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]`

**a)** Find the orthogonal projection matrix $P$ onto the subspace $W$ spanned by $\mathbf{v}_1 = (1, 1, 0)^T$ and $\mathbf{v}_2 = (0, 1, 1)^T$. Verify that $P$ is symmetric and idempotent. **(7 marks)**

**b)** Using the projection matrix obtained in part (a), project the vector $\mathbf{x} = (6, 3, 2)^T$ onto $W$. Also find the error vector and show that it is orthogonal to $W$. **(7 marks)**

### Model Solution for Question A:

#### Part (a) — Construction of Projection Matrix (7 marks)

**Step 1:** Form the matrix $A$ with $\mathbf{v}_1, \mathbf{v}_2$ as columns:

$$
A = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 \\ 1 & 1 \\ 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step 2:** Compute $A^T A$:

$$
A^T A = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 1 & 0 \\ 0 & 1 & 1 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 \\ 1 & 1 \\ 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 2 & 1 \\ 1 & 2 \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step 3:** Compute the determinant:

$$
\det(A^T A) = (2)(2) - (1)(1) = 3
$$

**Step 4:** Compute the inverse $(A^TA)^{-1}$:

$$
(A^T A)^{-1} = \frac{1}{3} \begin{bmatrix} 2 & -1 \\ -1 & 2 \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step 5:** Compute $A (A^TA)^{-1}$:

$$
A (A^T A)^{-1} = \frac{1}{3} \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 \\ 1 & 1 \\ 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} 2 & -1 \\ -1 & 2 \end{bmatrix} = \frac{1}{3} \begin{bmatrix} 2 & -1 \\ 1 & 1 \\ -1 & 2 \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step 6:** Compute $P = A(A^TA)^{-1}A^T$:

$$
P = \frac{1}{3} \begin{bmatrix} 2 & -1 \\ 1 & 1 \\ -1 & 2 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 1 & 0 \\ 0 & 1 & 1 \end{bmatrix} = \frac{1}{3} \begin{bmatrix} 2 & 1 & -1 \\ 1 & 2 & 1 \\ -1 & 1 & 2 \end{bmatrix}
$$

$$
\boxed{P = \frac{1}{3} \begin{bmatrix} 2 & 1 & -1 \\ 1 & 2 & 1 \\ -1 & 1 & 2 \end{bmatrix}}
$$

**Step 7: Verify Symmetry $P^T = P$:**

Since $P$ has the form $\frac{1}{3} M$ where $M_{ij} = M_{ji}$ (e.g., $M_{12} = 1 = M_{21}$, $M_{13} = -1 = M_{31}$), clearly $P^T = P$. ✓

**Step 8: Verify Idempotency $P^2 = P$:**

First, note that $P \cdot 3 = M$. Compute $M^2$:

$$
M^2 = \begin{bmatrix} 2 & 1 & -1 \\ 1 & 2 & 1 \\ -1 & 1 & 2 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} 2 & 1 & -1 \\ 1 & 2 & 1 \\ -1 & 1 & 2 \end{bmatrix}
$$

Computing element-by-element — Row 1:

$$
(2)(2) + (1)(1) + (-1)(-1) = 4 + 1 + 1 = 6
$$

$$
(2)(1) + (1)(2) + (-1)(1) = 2 + 2 - 1 = 3
$$

$$
(2)(-1) + (1)(1) + (-1)(2) = -2 + 1 - 2 = -3
$$

Computing element-by-element — Row 2:

$$
(1)(2) + (2)(1) + (1)(-1) = 2 + 2 - 1 = 3
$$

$$
(1)(1) + (2)(2) + (1)(1) = 1 + 4 + 1 = 6
$$

$$
(1)(-1) + (2)(1) + (1)(2) = -1 + 2 + 2 = 3
$$

Computing element-by-element — Row 3:

$$
(-1)(2) + (1)(1) + (2)(-1) = -2 + 1 - 2 = -3
$$

$$
(-1)(1) + (1)(2) + (2)(1) = -1 + 2 + 2 = 3
$$

$$
(-1)(-1) + (1)(1) + (2)(2) = 1 + 1 + 4 = 6
$$

Therefore:

$$
M^2 = \begin{bmatrix} 6 & 3 & -3 \\ 3 & 6 & 3 \\ -3 & 3 & 6 \end{bmatrix} = 3 \begin{bmatrix} 2 & 1 & -1 \\ 1 & 2 & 1 \\ -1 & 1 & 2 \end{bmatrix} = 3M
$$

Thus:

$$
P^2 = \frac{1}{3} M \cdot \frac{1}{3} M = \frac{1}{9} M^2 = \frac{1}{9} \cdot 3M = \frac{1}{3} M = P \quad \checkmark
$$

#### Part (b) — Projection of $\mathbf{x}$ and Error Analysis (7 marks)

**Step 1:** Compute $\hat{\mathbf{x}} = P \mathbf{x}$:

$$
\hat{\mathbf{x}} = \frac{1}{3} \begin{bmatrix} 2 & 1 & -1 \\ 1 & 2 & 1 \\ -1 & 1 & 2 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} 6 \\ 3 \\ 2 \end{bmatrix} = \frac{1}{3} \begin{bmatrix} 12 + 3 - 2 \\ 6 + 6 + 2 \\ -6 + 3 + 4 \end{bmatrix} = \frac{1}{3} \begin{bmatrix} 13 \\ 14 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix}
$$

$$
\boxed{\hat{\mathbf{x}} = \left( \frac{13}{3}, \frac{14}{3}, \frac{1}{3} \right)^T}
$$

**Step 2:** Compute the error vector $\mathbf{e} = \mathbf{x} - \hat{\mathbf{x}}$:

$$
\mathbf{e} = \begin{bmatrix} 6 \\ 3 \\ 2 \end{bmatrix} - \frac{1}{3} \begin{bmatrix} 13 \\ 14 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix} = \frac{1}{3} \begin{bmatrix} 18 - 13 \\ 9 - 14 \\ 6 - 1 \end{bmatrix} = \frac{1}{3} \begin{bmatrix} 5 \\ -5 \\ 5 \end{bmatrix}
$$

$$
\boxed{\mathbf{e} = \left( \frac{5}{3}, -\frac{5}{3}, \frac{5}{3} \right)^T}
$$

**Step 3:** Verify orthogonality $\mathbf{v}_1^T \mathbf{e} = 0$ and $\mathbf{v}_2^T \mathbf{e} = 0$:

$$
\mathbf{v}_1^T \mathbf{e} = (1)\left(\frac{5}{3}\right) + (1)\left(-\frac{5}{3}\right) + (0)\left(\frac{5}{3}\right) = \frac{5 - 5 + 0}{3} = 0 \quad \checkmark
$$

$$
\mathbf{v}_2^T \mathbf{e} = (0)\left(\frac{5}{3}\right) + (1)\left(-\frac{5}{3}\right) + (1)\left(\frac{5}{3}\right) = \frac{0 - 5 + 5}{3} = 0 \quad \checkmark
$$

The error vector is orthogonal to both basis vectors, hence orthogonal to $W$. The result is consistent with the property $(I - P)\mathbf{x} \perp W$.

---

## **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`

**a)** State and prove the **minimum distance property** of the orthogonal projection. Use a clear geometric figure-based explanation. **(7 marks)**

**b)** Consider the data matrix $X \in \mathbb{R}^{3 \times 2}$ where $\mathbf{x}_1 = (2, 0, 0)^T$ and $\mathbf{x}_2 = (1, 1, 1)^T$ are two observations. Find the orthogonal projection of the vector $\mathbf{y} = (1, 2, 3)^T$ onto the column space of $X$. What is the geometric meaning of the result in the context of **least-squares regression**? **(7 marks)**

### Model Solution for Question B:

#### Part (a) — Minimum Distance Property (7 marks)

**Statement:** Let $W$ be a subspace of $\mathbb{R}^n$ and $P$ be the orthogonal projection matrix onto $W$. For any vector $\mathbf{x} \in \mathbb{R}^n$, the projection $\hat{\mathbf{x}} = P\mathbf{x}$ satisfies:

$$
\Vert \mathbf{x} - \hat{\mathbf{x}} \Vert = \min_{\mathbf{w} \in W} \Vert \mathbf{x} - \mathbf{w} \Vert
$$

That is, $\hat{\mathbf{x}}$ is the unique vector in $W$ closest to $\mathbf{x}$.

**Geometric Explanation:** Imagine a vector $\mathbf{x}$ "floating" above a plane $W$. Among all points on the plane, the closest one is directly beneath $\mathbf{x}$ (along the perpendicular). This is the orthogonal projection.

**Proof:** Let $\mathbf{w} \in W$ be arbitrary. Since both $\hat{\mathbf{x}} = P\mathbf{x}$ and $\mathbf{w}$ are in $W$, the difference $\mathbf{z} = \hat{\mathbf{x}} - \mathbf{w} \in W$.

By the orthogonality property, the error $\mathbf{e} = \mathbf{x} - \hat{\mathbf{x}}$ is perpendicular to every vector in $W$, including $\mathbf{z}$:

$$
\mathbf{e}^T \mathbf{z} = 0
$$

Apply the Pythagorean theorem to the right triangle formed by $\mathbf{e}$ and $\mathbf{z}$:

$$
\Vert \mathbf{x} - \mathbf{w} \Vert^2 = \Vert \mathbf{e} + \mathbf{z} \Vert^2 = \Vert \mathbf{e} \Vert^2 + 2 \mathbf{e}^T \mathbf{z} + \Vert \mathbf{z} \Vert^2
$$

The cross term vanishes:

$$
\Vert \mathbf{x} - \mathbf{w} \Vert^2 = \Vert \mathbf{e} \Vert^2 + \Vert \mathbf{z} \Vert^2
$$

Since $\Vert \mathbf{z} \Vert^2 \geq 0$:

$$
\Vert \mathbf{x} - \mathbf{w} \Vert^2 \geq \Vert \mathbf{e} \Vert^2
$$

Taking square roots:

$$
\boxed{\Vert \mathbf{x} - \mathbf{w} \Vert \geq \Vert \mathbf{x} - \hat{\mathbf{x}} \Vert \quad \text{for all } \mathbf{w} \in W}
$$

**Uniqueness:** Equality holds iff $\mathbf{z} = \mathbf{0}$, i.e., $\mathbf{w} = \hat{\mathbf{x}}$, so the minimum is unique. $\blacksquare$

#### Part (b) — Projection onto Column Space (7 marks)

**Step 1:** Form the data matrix $X$ with $\mathbf{x}_1, \mathbf{x}_2$ as columns:

$$
X = \begin{bmatrix} 2 & 1 \\ 0 & 1 \\ 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step 2:** Compute $X^T X$:

$$
X^T X = \begin{bmatrix} 2 & 0 & 0 \\ 1 & 1 & 1 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} 2 & 1 \\ 0 & 1 \\ 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 4 & 2 \\ 2 & 3 \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step 3:** Compute $\det(X^T X) = (4)(3) - (2)(2) = 12 - 4 = 8$.

**Step 4:** Compute $(X^T X)^{-1}$:

$$
(X^T X)^{-1} = \frac{1}{8} \begin{bmatrix} 3 & -2 \\ -2 & 4 \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step 5:** Compute $X^T \mathbf{y}$:

$$
X^T \mathbf{y} = \begin{bmatrix} 2 & 0 & 0 \\ 1 & 1 & 1 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} 1 \\ 2 \\ 3 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 2 \\ 6 \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step 6:** Solve normal equations $\mathbf{c} = (X^T X)^{-1} X^T \mathbf{y}$:

$$
\mathbf{c} = \frac{1}{8} \begin{bmatrix} 3 & -2 \\ -2 & 4 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} 2 \\ 6 \end{bmatrix} = \frac{1}{8} \begin{bmatrix} 6 - 12 \\ -4 + 24 \end{bmatrix} = \frac{1}{8} \begin{bmatrix} -6 \\ 20 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} -3/4 \\ 5/2 \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step 7:** Compute the projection $\hat{\mathbf{y}} = X \mathbf{c}$:

$$
\hat{\mathbf{y}} = \begin{bmatrix} 2 & 1 \\ 0 & 1 \\ 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} -3/4 \\ 5/2 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} -3/2 + 5/2 \\ 5/2 \\ 5/2 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 1 \\ 5/2 \\ 5/2 \end{bmatrix}
$$

$$
\boxed{\hat{\mathbf{y}} = \left( 1, \frac{5}{2}, \frac{5}{2} \right)^T}
$$

**Step 8:** Compute the error $\mathbf{e} = \mathbf{y} - \hat{\mathbf{y}}$:

$$
\mathbf{e} = \begin{bmatrix} 1 \\ 2 \\ 3 \end{bmatrix} - \begin{bmatrix} 1 \\ 5/2 \\ 5/2 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ -1/2 \\ 1/2 \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Geometric Meaning in Least-Squares Regression:**

In the linear regression model $\mathbf{y} \approx X\boldsymbol{\beta}$, the **best-fit coefficient vector** is $\boldsymbol{\beta} = \mathbf{c} = (-3/4, 5/2)^T$. The projection $\hat{\mathbf{y}}$ represents the **fitted values** (the model's predictions), and the error $\mathbf{e}$ is the **residual vector** — the part of $\mathbf{y}$ that the model cannot explain.

By the **minimum distance property**, this $\hat{\mathbf{y}}$ is the closest point in the column space of $X$ to $\mathbf{y}$, which means the sum of squared residuals $\Vert \mathbf{e} \Vert^2 = 0 + 1/4 + 1/4 = 1/2$ is **minimized** over all possible coefficient vectors. This is precisely the **Least Squares Method** used universally in linear regression.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls to Avoid:**
>
> 1. **Confusing orthogonal and oblique projections:** If $P^T \neq P$, the projection is *oblique*, not orthogonal. Always check symmetry.
> 2. **Forgetting to verify rank conditions:** The formula $P = A(A^TA)^{-1}A^T$ requires $A$ to have full column rank. If $A$ is rank-deficient, $(A^TA)^{-1}$ does not exist.
> 3. **Sign errors in $A^TA$ inverse:** When computing $(A^TA)^{-1}$, swapping the diagonal and negating the off-diagonal is correct for $2 \times 2$, but be careful with the **scalar factor** $\frac{1}{\det}$.
> 4. **Skipping orthogonality verification:** Many students compute $\hat{\mathbf{x}}$ but forget to verify that $\mathbf{v}_i^T \mathbf{e} = 0$ — a key step examiners look for.
> 5. **Pythagorean theorem step:** In the minimum distance proof, students often drop the $2\mathbf{e}^T\mathbf{z}$ cross term without explaining that it equals zero due to orthogonality. Always state *why* it vanishes.
> 6. **Matrix multiplication order:** $QQ^T \neq Q^TQ$ in general. The projection is $QQ^T$ (not $Q^TQ$).

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Definition:** An orthogonal projection is a linear map $P$ satisfying $P^2 = P$ (idempotent) and $P^T = P$ (symmetric).
- **Three Formula Variants:**
  - Line: $P = \dfrac{\mathbf{v}\mathbf{v}^T}{\mathbf{v}^T\mathbf{v}}$
  - Orthonormal basis: $P = QQ^T$
  - General basis: $P = A(A^TA)^{-1}A^T$
- **Geometric Intuition:** The projection of $\mathbf{x}$ onto $W$ is the **closest point in $W$ to $\mathbf{x}$**; the connecting segment is perpendicular to $W$.
- **Error Vector:** $\mathbf{e} = \mathbf{x} - P\mathbf{x}$ satisfies $\mathbf{e} \perp W$ and $\Vert \mathbf{e} \Vert$ is the minimum distance.
- **Eigenvalues:** All eigenvalues of a projection matrix are either $0$ or $1$, with $\text{tr}(P) = \text{rank}(P) = \dim(W)$.
- **Rejection Operator:** $I - P$ projects onto the **orthogonal complement** $W^{\perp}$.
- **Normal Equations:** The optimal coefficients in least-squares regression come from $A^T A \mathbf{c} = A^T \mathbf{y}$.
- **AI/ML Relevance:** PCA, regression, neural-network weight initialization, low-rank approximation, and bias removal in embeddings all rely on orthogonal projection.
- **Numerical Tip:** Always prefer orthonormal basis ($P = QQ^T$) over general basis ($P = A(A^TA)^{-1}A^T$) for numerical stability.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Importance of Least Squares, Determinants – Properties of Determinants
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAET753 - LINEAR ALGEBRA IN AI AND ML
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Importance of Least Squares, Determinants – Properties of Determinants

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Importance of Least Squares

### Formal Definition
The **Least Squares Method** is an optimization technique used to find the *best-fitting* curve or linear model to a given set of data points by **minimizing the sum of the squares of the residuals** (vertical differences between the observed values and the values predicted by the model). In the matrix formulation, given an overdetermined system $\mathbf{A}\mathbf{x} = \mathbf{b}$ with more equations than unknowns, the least squares solution is the vector $\hat{\mathbf{x}}$ that minimizes $\|\mathbf{A}\mathbf{x} - \mathbf{b}\|^2$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why "Squares"?** The squaring serves two critical mathematical purposes:
> 1. It eliminates sign cancellation (errors above and below the line don't cancel out).
> 2. It produces a *convex, differentiable* objective function — which guarantees a unique global minimum.

### Intuition (Real-World Analogy)
Imagine a group of **10 students** attempting to guess the weight of a watermelon in a school fair. Their guesses scatter above and below the true weight. If you just *average* their guesses, positive and negative errors cancel — but what if the errors are skewed? By **squaring** the deviations, you penalize large mistakes disproportionately (a guess off by 5 kg contributes **25** to the penalty, not 5). This is exactly what the Least Squares method does: it gives a *best-fit* line through noisy scattered data, the same line that powers **Linear Regression in Machine Learning**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Connection to AI/ML:** Almost every classical ML model — Linear Regression, Logistic Regression (via cross-entropy), Ridge/Lasso Regression, Support Vector Regression, and even the backpropagation in Neural Networks — has Least Squares minimization at its heart.

### Why Least Squares is Indispensable in AI/ML

| Real-World Scenario | Role of Least Squares |
|---|---|
| **House Price Prediction** | Fits a hyperplane to features (area, rooms, location) minimizing squared error. |
| **Sensor Calibration** | Recovers true signal from noisy measurements. |
| **Curve Fitting in Physics/Engineering** | Determines coefficients of polynomial/exponential models. |
| **Computer Vision (Pose Estimation)** | Solves overdetermined projection equations. |
| **Recommender Systems** | Matrix factorization via squared error loss. |
| **GPS & Triangulation** | Pseudorange equations solved via least squares. |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The phrase "**In AI and ML**" in your course title justifies why this section appears before the linear algebra toolkit. Modern ML is fundamentally a *problem of minimizing loss functions*, and squared-error loss is the oldest, most theoretically grounded, and most widely used.

---

## 1.2 Determinants – Properties of Determinants

### Formal Definition
The **determinant** is a *scalar-valued function* of a square matrix $\mathbf{A}_{n \times n}$, denoted $\det(\mathbf{A})$ or $\vert \mathbf{A} \vert$, that encodes the **signed hypervolume** of the parallelepiped spanned by the column (or row) vectors of the matrix. A determinant of **zero** signals that the matrix is **singular** (its columns are linearly dependent, meaning the transformation collapses the dimension).

### Intuition (Real-World Analogy)
Think of a $2 \times 2$ matrix as a transformation that takes the **unit square** (area = 1) to a new parallelogram. The determinant tells you the **signed area** of that new parallelogram:
- $\det = 2$ means the area *doubled*.
- $\det = -1$ means the square was *flipped and preserved in size* (a reflection).
- $\det = 0$ means the entire square *collapsed onto a line or point* — information is lost (singular matrix → non-invertible).

For a $3 \times 3$ matrix, this generalizes to **signed volume** of a parallelepiped.

> [!NOTE]
> **Geometric Role:** $\vert \det(\mathbf{A}) \vert$ = scale factor of the linear transformation. $\text{sign}(\det(\mathbf{A}))$ = orientation (clockwise vs. counterclockwise).

### Where Determinants Are Used in AI/ML
1. **Checking Linear Independence:** $\det \neq 0 \iff$ features are linearly independent.
2. **Invertibility Test:** $\mathbf{A}^{-1}$ exists $\iff \det(\mathbf{A}) \neq 0$.
3. **Jacobian Determinant:** Used in normalizing flows and change-of-variable formulas in probabilistic models.
4. **Eigenvalue Link:** $\det(\mathbf{A}) = \prod_{i=1}^{n} \lambda_i$ (product of eigenvalues).
5. **Volume Computation in Probability:** For Gaussian distributions, $\det(\Sigma)$ appears in the normalization constant.
6. **Multicollinearity Detection in Regression:** Near-zero $\det(\mathbf{X}^T\mathbf{X})$ warns of dependent features.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The "Why" Behind Least Squares

The goal is to solve the overdetermined system $\mathbf{A}\mathbf{x} = \mathbf{b}$ where $\mathbf{A} \in \mathbb{R}^{m \times n}$ with $m > n$. A perfect solution rarely exists, so we seek $\hat{\mathbf{x}}$ that minimizes the squared Euclidean norm of the residual:

$$J(\mathbf{x}) = \|\mathbf{A}\mathbf{x} - \mathbf{b}\|^2 = (\mathbf{A}\mathbf{x} - \mathbf{b})^T(\mathbf{A}\mathbf{x} - \mathbf{b})$$

### Logical Derivation Path
1. **Expand** the objective function.
2. **Differentiate** w.r.t. $\mathbf{x}$ and set to zero (first-order optimality).
3. Arrive at the **Normal Equations**: $\mathbf{A}^T\mathbf{A}\mathbf{x} = \mathbf{A}^T\mathbf{b}$.
4. **Solution:** $\hat{\mathbf{x}} = (\mathbf{A}^T\mathbf{A})^{-1}\mathbf{A}^T\mathbf{b}$, where $(\mathbf{A}^T\mathbf{A})^{-1}\mathbf{A}^T$ is the **Moore-Penrose pseudoinverse** $\mathbf{A}^+$.

### Why "Squared Error" and Not Absolute Error?
- **Differentiability:** $\vert e \vert$ has a non-differentiable kink at 0; $e^2$ is smooth everywhere.
- **Convexity:** $e^2$ is strictly convex → unique global minimum.
- **Statistical Optimality (Gauss-Markov Theorem):** Under zero-mean, equal-variance, uncorrelated errors, the least squares estimator is the **Best Linear Unbiased Estimator (BLUE)**.
- **Closed-form Solution:** Linear least squares yields a *direct formula*; $L_1$ (absolute) requires iterative methods.

### Engineering Utility
- **Linear Regression Models** in ML.
- **Kalman Filters** in robotics and control.
- **Signal Processing:** Wiener filtering.
- **Computer Graphics:** Curve/surface fitting.

---

## 2.2 The "Why" Behind Determinant Properties

Determinants obey a rich set of algebraic rules that simplify computations. These properties let us evaluate determinants of *large* matrices by *row/column reduction* instead of full cofactor expansion (which is $O(n!)$ — practically infeasible beyond $n=10$).

### Geometric Justification of Each Property
- **Row scaling by $k$**: Multiplies the parallelepiped volume by $k$.
- **Row swap**: Reverses orientation → sign flips.
- **Row of zeros**: Volume = 0.
- **Adding a multiple of one row to another**: Shear — preserves volume.

---

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### Least Squares Formulas

| Formula / Equation | Meaning / Condition |
|---|---|
| $J(\mathbf{x}) = \sum_{i=1}^{m}(a_i^T x - b_i)^2$ | Scalar objective (sum of squared residuals) |
| $J(\mathbf{x}) = \|\mathbf{A}\mathbf{x} - \mathbf{b}\|_2^2$ | Matrix-vector norm form |
| $\mathbf{A}^T\mathbf{A}\hat{\mathbf{x}} = \mathbf{A}^T\mathbf{b}$ | Normal Equations |
| $\hat{\mathbf{x}} = (\mathbf{A}^T\mathbf{A})^{-1}\mathbf{A}^T\mathbf{b}$ | Closed-form LS solution |
| $\mathbf{A}^+ = (\mathbf{A}^T\mathbf{A})^{-1}\mathbf{A}^T$ | Pseudoinverse (full column rank) |
| $\hat{\mathbf{b}} = \mathbf{A}\hat{\mathbf{x}}$ | Projected (fitted) values |
| $\mathbf{r} = \mathbf{b} - \hat{\mathbf{b}}$ | Residual vector |
| $\mathbf{A}^T\mathbf{r} = \mathbf{0}$ | Orthogonality condition |
| $\text{SSE} = \mathbf{b}^T\mathbf{b} - \hat{\mathbf{x}}^T\mathbf{A}^T\mathbf{b}$ | Sum of squared errors |
| $R^2 = 1 - \frac{\text{SSE}}{\text{SST}}$ | Coefficient of determination |

### Determinant Properties

| # | Property | Formula | Geometric Meaning |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Identity matrix | $\det(\mathbf{I}) = 1$ | Unit volume preserved |
| 2 | Transpose | $\det(\mathbf{A}^T) = \det(\mathbf{A})$ | Volume = Volume |
| 3 | Product rule | $\det(\mathbf{AB}) = \det(\mathbf{A})\det(\mathbf{B})$ | Composition multiplies volumes |
| 4 | Scalar multiple | $\det(k\mathbf{A}) = k^n \det(\mathbf{A})$ | Each of $n$ rows scaled |
| 5 | Inverse | $\det(\mathbf{A}^{-1}) = \frac{1}{\det(\mathbf{A})}$ | Volume inverts |
| 6 | Power | $\det(\mathbf{A}^k) = (\det(\mathbf{A}))^k$ | Repeated transformation |
| 7 | Row swap | $\det$ flips sign | Orientation reversal |
| 8 | Row scaling | Row $\times k \Rightarrow \det \times k$ | Volume scaling |
| 9 | Row addition | $\mathbf{R}_i + k\mathbf{R}_j \Rightarrow \det$ unchanged | Shear preserves volume |
| 10 | Zero row/column | $\det = 0$ | Flattened parallelepiped |
| 11 | Duplicate rows | $\det = 0$ | Linearly dependent |
| 12 | Triangular matrix | $\det = \prod_i a_{ii}$ | Product of diagonal |
| 13 | Singular matrix | $\det(\mathbf{A}) = 0 \iff \mathbf{A}$ not invertible | Lost invertibility |
| 14 | Eigenvalue product | $\det(\mathbf{A}) = \prod_{i=1}^{n} \lambda_i$ | Spectral characterization |
| 15 | Cofactor expansion | $\det(\mathbf{A}) = \sum_j (-1)^{i+j} a_{ij} M_{ij}$ | Definition (Laplace) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** For $3 \times 3$ and higher, *never* use the brute-force cofactor expansion if the problem gives a structured matrix. Use **row/column operations (Properties 7–9)** to reduce to triangular form and then apply Property 12. This is the fastest method and the one examiners expect.

### Special Determinant Formulas

| Matrix Type | Determinant Formula |
|---|---|
| $2 \times 2$: $\begin{bmatrix} a & b \\ c & d \end{bmatrix}$ | $ad - bc$ |
| $3 \times 3$: Diagonal | $a_{11} a_{22} a_{33}$ |
| Block triangular: $\begin{bmatrix} \mathbf{A} & \mathbf{B} \\ \mathbf{0} & \mathbf{D} \end{bmatrix}$ | $\det(\mathbf{A}) \cdot \det(\mathbf{D})$ |
| Vandermonde: $V = [x_j^{i-1}]$ | $\prod_{1 \le j < k \le n} (x_k - x_j)$ |

> [!NOTE]
> **Note on Notation:** I have used $\vert \cdot \vert$ for determinant only when the context (square matrix) makes it unambiguous. In LaTeX, always write `\det(\mathbf{A})` to avoid confusion with absolute value.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Least Squares Solution

**Given:** $\mathbf{A} \in \mathbb{R}^{m \times n}$ with $m > n$ (more equations than unknowns), $\mathbf{b} \in \mathbb{R}^m$.

**Goal:** Find $\hat{\mathbf{x}}$ that minimizes $J(\mathbf{x}) = \|\mathbf{A}\mathbf{x} - \mathbf{b}\|^2$.

### Step 1: Write the Objective Function in Expanded Form

$$
\begin{aligned}
J(\mathbf{x}) &= (\mathbf{A}\mathbf{x} - \mathbf{b})^T(\mathbf{A}\mathbf{x} - \mathbf{b}) \\
&= [(\mathbf{A}\mathbf{x})^T - \mathbf{b}^T][\mathbf{A}\mathbf{x} - \mathbf{b}] \\
&= \mathbf{x}^T\mathbf{A}^T\mathbf{A}\mathbf{x} - \mathbf{x}^T\mathbf{A}^T\mathbf{b} - \mathbf{b}^T\mathbf{A}\mathbf{x} + \mathbf{b}^T\mathbf{b}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Logic:** The two middle terms are scalars, so $\mathbf{x}^T\mathbf{A}^T\mathbf{b} = (\mathbf{x}^T\mathbf{A}^T\mathbf{b})^T = \mathbf{b}^T\mathbf{A}\mathbf{x}$.

### Step 2: Differentiate with Respect to $\mathbf{x}$

Using the standard matrix calculus identities:
- $\frac{\partial}{\partial \mathbf{x}} \mathbf{x}^T\mathbf{M}\mathbf{x} = 2\mathbf{M}\mathbf{x}$ (when $\mathbf{M}$ is symmetric)
- $\frac{\partial}{\partial \mathbf{x}} \mathbf{c}^T\mathbf{x} = \mathbf{c}$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{\partial J}{\partial \mathbf{x}} &= 2\mathbf{A}^T\mathbf{A}\mathbf{x} - 2\mathbf{A}^T\mathbf{b}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 3: Set the Gradient to Zero (First-Order Optimality)

$$
\begin{aligned}
2\mathbf{A}^T\mathbf{A}\hat{\mathbf{x}} - 2\mathbf{A}^T\mathbf{b} &= \mathbf{0} \\
\mathbf{A}^T\mathbf{A}\hat{\mathbf{x}} &= \mathbf{A}^T\mathbf{b}
\end{aligned}
$$

This is the **Normal Equation**.

### Step 4: Solve for $\hat{\mathbf{x}}$ Assuming $\mathbf{A}^T\mathbf{A}$ is Invertible

$$
\begin{aligned}
\hat{\mathbf{x}} &= (\mathbf{A}^T\mathbf{A})^{-1}\mathbf{A}^T\mathbf{b} \\
&= \mathbf{A}^+ \mathbf{b}
\end{aligned}
$$

where $\mathbf{A}^+ = (\mathbf{A}^T\mathbf{A})^{-1}\mathbf{A}^T$ is the **pseudoinverse**.

### Step 5: Verify the Minimum Using the Hessian

$$
\begin{aligned}
\mathbf{H} = \frac{\partial^2 J}{\partial \mathbf{x}^2} = 2\mathbf{A}^T\mathbf{A}
\end{aligned}
$$

Since $\mathbf{A}^T\mathbf{A}$ is positive semi-definite (and positive definite if $\mathbf{A}$ has full column rank), the Hessian is positive definite, confirming a **unique global minimum**.

---

## 3.2 Worked Example: Linear Regression via Least Squares

**Problem:** Fit a line $y = \beta_0 + \beta_1 x$ to the data points $(1, 2), (2, 3), (3, 5), (4, 4)$ using least squares.

### Step 1: Construct $\mathbf{A}$ and $\mathbf{b}$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\mathbf{A} &= \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 1 \\ 1 & 2 \\ 1 & 3 \\ 1 & 4 \end{bmatrix}, \quad \mathbf{b} = \begin{bmatrix} 2 \\ 3 \\ 5 \\ 4 \end{bmatrix}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 2: Compute $\mathbf{A}^T\mathbf{A}$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\mathbf{A}^T\mathbf{A} &= \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 1 & 1 & 1 \\ 1 & 2 & 3 & 4 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 1 \\ 1 & 2 \\ 1 & 3 \\ 1 & 4 \end{bmatrix} \\
&= \begin{bmatrix} 4 & 10 \\ 10 & 30 \end{bmatrix}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Row-by-row:** $(1,1): 1+1+1+1 = 4$. $(1,2): 1+2+3+4 = 10$. $(2,2): 1+4+9+16 = 30$.

### Step 3: Compute $\mathbf{A}^T\mathbf{b}$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\mathbf{A}^T\mathbf{b} &= \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 1 & 1 & 1 \\ 1 & 2 & 3 & 4 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} 2 \\ 3 \\ 5 \\ 4 \end{bmatrix} \\
&= \begin{bmatrix} 2+3+5+4 \\ 2+6+15+16 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 14 \\ 39 \end{bmatrix}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 4: Solve the Normal Equation

$$
\begin{aligned}
\begin{bmatrix} 4 & 10 \\ 10 & 30 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} \beta_0 \\ \beta_1 \end{bmatrix} &= \begin{bmatrix} 14 \\ 39 \end{bmatrix}
\end{aligned}
$$

Determinant of coefficient matrix: $4 \cdot 30 - 10 \cdot 10 = 120 - 100 = 20$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\begin{bmatrix} \beta_0 \\ \beta_1 \end{bmatrix} &= \frac{1}{20} \begin{bmatrix} 30 & -10 \\ -10 & 4 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} 14 \\ 39 \end{bmatrix} \\
&= \frac{1}{20} \begin{bmatrix} 30 \cdot 14 - 10 \cdot 39 \\ -10 \cdot 14 + 4 \cdot 39 \end{bmatrix} \\
&= \frac{1}{20} \begin{bmatrix} 420 - 390 \\ -140 + 156 \end{bmatrix} \\
&= \frac{1}{20} \begin{bmatrix} 30 \\ 16 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 1.5 \\ 0.8 \end{bmatrix}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 5: Final Fitted Line

$$
\begin{aligned}
\hat{y} = 1.5 + 0.8 x
\end{aligned}
$$

The best-fit line: $y = 1.5 + 0.8x$.

---

## 3.3 Determinant: Exhaustive Derivation of Key Properties

### Property 1: $\det(\mathbf{I}) = 1$

**By definition** (Laplace expansion along the first row of $\mathbf{I}_n$):
- The first row has a single $1$ at position $(1,1)$, and the corresponding minor is $\mathbf{I}_{n-1}$.
- By induction on $n$, the determinant of $\mathbf{I}_n$ is $1$.

**Verification for $n=2$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\det\begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 \\ 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix} = (1)(1) - (0)(0) = 1 \;\checkmark
\end{aligned}
$$

### Property 2: $\det(\mathbf{A}^T) = \det(\mathbf{A})$

**Proof sketch via cofactor expansion:**
The cofactor $C_{ij}$ in $\mathbf{A}$ is computed from the minor $M_{ij}$ (the submatrix obtained by deleting row $i$ and column $j$). When we transpose, the $(i,j)$ minor in $\mathbf{A}$ becomes the $(j,i)$ minor in $\mathbf{A}^T$. Since the $(j,i)$ cofactor expansion in $\mathbf{A}^T$ and the $(i,j)$ cofactor expansion in $\mathbf{A}$ traverse the *same set of minors*, the values match.

> **Geometric Intuition:** Row-vectors and column-vectors span the *same parallelepiped* — only orientation convention differs. The absolute value of the volume is identical, and transpose does not flip orientation.

### Property 3: $\det(\mathbf{AB}) = \det(\mathbf{A})\det(\mathbf{B})$

**Proof outline:**
- View $\mathbf{A}\mathbf{B}$ as a transformation. $\mathbf{B}$ first transforms a unit cube to a parallelepiped of volume $\det(\mathbf{B})$. Then $\mathbf{A}$ scales that by $\det(\mathbf{A})$. Total volume scaling: $\det(\mathbf{A}) \det(\mathbf{B})$.

**Verification with $2 \times 2$ matrices:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\mathbf{A} = \begin{bmatrix} a & b \\ c & d \end{bmatrix}, \quad \mathbf{B} = \begin{bmatrix} p & q \\ r & s \end{bmatrix}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\mathbf{A}\mathbf{B} &= \begin{bmatrix} ap+br & aq+bs \\ cp+dr & cq+ds \end{bmatrix} \\
\det(\mathbf{A}\mathbf{B}) &= (ap+br)(cq+ds) - (aq+bs)(cp+dr)
\end{aligned}
$$

Expanding both products:

$$
\begin{aligned}
(ap+br)(cq+ds) &= apcq + apds + brcq + brds \\
(aq+bs)(cp+dr) &= aqcp + aqdr + bscp + bsdr
\end{aligned}
$$

Subtracting:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\det(\mathbf{AB}) &= apds + brcq - aqdr - bscp \\
&= adps - adqr + bcrq - bcsp \quad \text{(rearranging)}\\
&= (ad - bc)(ps - qr) \\
&= \det(\mathbf{A}) \cdot \det(\mathbf{B}) \;\checkmark
\end{aligned}
$$

### Property 4: $\det(k\mathbf{A}) = k^n \det(\mathbf{A})$

**Proof:** Multiplying the matrix by $k$ scales *every entry* by $k$, which is equivalent to scaling *each row* by $k$. Each row scaling multiplies the determinant by $k$ (Property 8). With $n$ rows, the total factor is $k^n$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\det(k\mathbf{A}) = \det\begin{bmatrix} ka_{11} & ka_{12} & \cdots \\ ka_{21} & ka_{22} & \cdots \\ \vdots & \vdots & \ddots \end{bmatrix} = k^n \det(\mathbf{A}) \;\checkmark
\end{aligned}
$$

### Property 5: $\det(\mathbf{A}^{-1}) = \frac{1}{\det(\mathbf{A})}$

**Proof:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\mathbf{A} \cdot \mathbf{A}^{-1} = \mathbf{I} \quad &\Rightarrow \quad \det(\mathbf{A}\mathbf{A}^{-1}) = \det(\mathbf{I}) = 1 \\
&\Rightarrow \quad \det(\mathbf{A}) \det(\mathbf{A}^{-1}) = 1 \quad \text{(using Property 3)} \\
&\Rightarrow \quad \det(\mathbf{A}^{-1}) = \frac{1}{\det(\mathbf{A})} \;\checkmark
\end{aligned}
$$

### Property 9: Row Operation $\mathbf{R}_i \to \mathbf{R}_i + k\mathbf{R}_j$ Preserves Determinant

**Proof:** Using multilinearity of the determinant in each row:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\det(\mathbf{A}') &= \det\begin{bmatrix} \vdots \\ \mathbf{R}_i + k\mathbf{R}_j \\ \vdots \end{bmatrix} \\
&= \det\begin{bmatrix} \vdots \\ \mathbf{R}_i \\ \vdots \end{bmatrix} + k \det\begin{bmatrix} \vdots \\ \mathbf{R}_j \\ \vdots \end{bmatrix}
\end{aligned}
$$

The second determinant is **zero** because two rows are identical. Hence:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\det(\mathbf{A}') = \det(\mathbf{A}) \;\checkmark
\end{aligned}
$$

---

## 3.4 Worked Example: Computing a $3 \times 3$ Determinant Using Properties

**Compute:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\mathbf{M} = \begin{bmatrix} 2 & 1 & 3 \\ 4 & 5 & 7 \\ 6 & 8 & 9 \end{bmatrix}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 1: Eliminate First Column Below Pivot

Apply $R_2 \to R_2 - 2R_1$ and $R_3 \to R_3 - 3R_1$ (does not change determinant):

$$
\begin{aligned}
R_2: \; [4, 5, 7] - 2[2, 1, 3] &= [4-4, \; 5-2, \; 7-6] = [0, 3, 1] \\
R_3: \; [6, 8, 9] - 3[2, 1, 3] &= [6-6, \; 8-3, \; 9-9] = [0, 5, 0]
\end{aligned}
$$

New matrix:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\mathbf{M}' = \begin{bmatrix} 2 & 1 & 3 \\ 0 & 3 & 1 \\ 0 & 5 & 0 \end{bmatrix}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 2: Eliminate Second Column Below Pivot

Apply $R_3 \to R_3 - \frac{5}{3} R_2$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
R_3: \; [0, 5, 0] - \tfrac{5}{3}[0, 3, 1] &= [0, \; 5-5, \; 0-\tfrac{5}{3}] = [0, 0, -\tfrac{5}{3}]
\end{aligned}
$$

Upper triangular form:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\mathbf{M}'' = \begin{bmatrix} 2 & 1 & 3 \\ 0 & 3 & 1 \\ 0 & 0 & -\tfrac{5}{3} \end{bmatrix}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 3: Multiply Diagonal Entries

$$
\begin{aligned}
\det(\mathbf{M}) = 2 \cdot 3 \cdot \left(-\tfrac{5}{3}\right) = 2 \cdot (-5) = -10
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Verification using cofactor expansion along column 1:**
> $\det(\mathbf{M}) = 2 \cdot (5 \cdot 9 - 7 \cdot 8) - 4 \cdot (1 \cdot 9 - 3 \cdot 8) + 6 \cdot (1 \cdot 7 - 3 \cdot 5)$
> $= 2 \cdot (45 - 56) - 4 \cdot (9 - 24) + 6 \cdot (7 - 15)$
> $= 2 \cdot (-11) - 4 \cdot (-15) + 6 \cdot (-8)$
> $= -22 + 60 - 48 = -10 \;\checkmark$

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Least Squares — Block-Level Functional Architecture

The following diagram maps the **operational pipeline** of a Least Squares solver inside an ML system.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Raw Data Points x_i, y_i] --> B[Construct Design Matrix A]
    BB[Construct Target Vector b] --> B
    B --> C[Form Normal Equations A^T A x = A^T b]
    C --> D{A^T A Invertible?}
    D -- Yes --> E[Compute Inverse of A^T A]
    D -- No --> F[Use SVD or Pseudoinverse]
    E --> G[Compute x_hat equals A^T A inverse A^T b]
    F --> G
    G --> H[Predicted Values y_hat = A x_hat]
    H --> I[Compute Residuals r = b - y_hat]
    I --> J[Minimize Sum of Squared Residuals]
    J --> K[Best Fit Model]
    K --> L[Deploy in ML Pipeline]
    L --> M[Prediction on New Data]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** The flowchart shows that the *square of errors* drives the optimization, the *normal equations* act as the bridge, and invertibility of $\mathbf{A}^T\mathbf{A}$ determines whether a direct or iterative path is taken. In modern ML, this is computed via QR decomposition or SVD instead of explicit inversion for **numerical stability**.

---

## 4.2 Determinant — Functional Properties Map

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P[Properties of Determinant] --> P1[Identity: det I equals 1]
    P --> P2[Transpose: det A^T equals det A]
    P --> P3[Product: det AB equals det A times det B]
    P --> P4[Scalar: det kA equals k to the n times det A]
    P --> P5[Inverse: det A inverse equals 1 over det A]
    P --> P6[Row Operations]
    P6 --> P6a[Swap: changes sign]
    P6 --> P6b[Scale row by k: multiplies by k]
    P6 --> P6c[Add multiple: no change]
    P --> P7[Triangular: product of diagonal]
    P --> P8[Singular: det equals 0]
    P8 --> P8a[No inverse exists]
    P8 --> P8b[Columns linearly dependent]
    P8 --> P8c[Zero volume in transformation]
```

---

## 4.3 Geometric Interpretation of the Determinant

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    G[Geometric Meaning of Determinant] --> G1[2x2 Matrix]
    G --> G2[3x3 Matrix]
    G --> G3[n x n Matrix]
    G1 --> G1a[Unit Square transformed to Parallelogram]
    G1a --> G1b[det equals Signed Area of Parallelogram]
    G2 --> G2a[Unit Cube transformed to Parallelepiped]
    G2a --> G2b[det equals Signed Volume of Parallelepiped]
    G3 --> G3a[Unit Hypercube transformed to Parallelotope]
    G3a --> G3b[det equals Signed Hypervolume]
    G1b --> H[Sign Convention]
    G2b --> H
    G3b --> H
    H --> H1[Positive: Orientation Preserved]
    H --> H2[Negative: Orientation Reversed]
    H --> H3[Zero: Dimension Collapsed]
```

---

## 4.4 Least Squares and Determinant — Integration in Linear Regression

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Q[Linear Regression using Least Squares] --> Q1[Data Set D with m points and n features]
    Q1 --> Q2[Form A in R to the m by n and b in R to the m]
    Q2 --> Q3[Build A transpose A]
    Q3 --> Q4[Check Determinant of A transpose A]
    Q4 --> Q5{Determinant nonzero?}
    Q5 -- Yes --> Q6[Invertible, Unique LS Solution Exists]
    Q5 -- No --> Q7[Multicollinearity: Features are Dependent]
    Q7 --> Q8[Apply Regularization Ridge or Lasso]
    Q6 --> Q9[Solve Normal Equations]
    Q8 --> Q9
    Q9 --> Q10[Estimated Coefficients beta hat]
    Q10 --> Q11[Compute Coefficient of Determination R squared]
    Q11 --> Q12[Model Validated for Deployment]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Crucial Linkage:** The **determinant of $\mathbf{A}^T\mathbf{A}$** is the *single scalar that tells you whether your regression problem is well-posed*. If it is zero (or near-zero due to floating-point errors), the matrix is singular and the normal equations have *infinitely many solutions* — which is precisely why techniques like **Ridge Regression** add a small $\lambda \mathbf{I}$ to $\mathbf{A}^T\mathbf{A}$ to make it invertible.
